title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark
text:
01Why participate
  Whether large language models can stand in for human participants in experiments is one of the most consequential open questions in behavioral science. The evidence so far is mixed and hard to interpret: most existing evaluations target human studies that were already published — and plausibly in the models' training data — and very few studies are credibly preregistered, so reported successes are entangled with researcher degrees of freedom in model and prompt choice.
  This benchmark study addresses these problems. The target is a genuinely novel megastudy: none of the 16 interventions has ever been tested in this specific form on the specific outcomes. The human data are held exclusively by the core team, and every prediction must be deposited and time-stamped before any human data are released.
  What you get out of it:
  
    A credible test of your approach. Your method is evaluated on truly out-of-sample human behavior, against a preregistered human–human comparison.
    Co-authorship on the benchmark paper. All participating teams join the resulting publication, which reports the full results table and a structured catalogue of approaches.
    A catalogue of the field. Each registered method becomes a structured, comparable entry describing how teams currently approach AI prediction of behavioral experiments — open- vs. closed-weight models, persona sources, administration protocols, fine-tuning, in-context learning — and which of these choices appear to matter.
    No single winner. Approaches are scored on a ladder of metrics of increasing strictness — from directional agreement to formal equivalence — plus distributional fidelity and stereotyping diagnostics. We expect different approaches to do well on different rungs; the point is to map the design space and compare approaches across metrics, not to crown one champion.
  
  
    The target study
    A between-subjects field experiment testing 16 text-based interventions to increase trust in climate scientists against a control condition. The sample is N ≈ 18,000 U.S. adults (1,000 per intervention plus 2,000 control), recruited from an opt-in panel under census-based cross quotas on gender × age and gender × race/ethnicity. The primary outcome is a 12-item multidimensional trust composite; secondary and tertiary outcomes span funding perceptions, policy attitudes, institutional trust, climate beliefs and behaviors, and two behavioral measures: a real-money donation decision and a newsletter sign-up. See the full instrument on the questionnaire page and the sample and quota detail in the benchmark preregistration.
  




  02Rules
  Any AI-based approach is welcome — any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, or prior literature — under a few firm ground rules.
  
    Blinding is absolute. No team may access, solicit, or be shown any human outcome data from this study before the prediction lock. A signed attestation is part of registration (item I.3 of the form).
    AI-based and automated. Pipelines must be AI-based and run without humans in the loop at prediction time. Human effort goes into designing the pipeline, not into adjusting its outputs.
    Otherwise unrestricted. Any models (open or closed weight), any prompting or persona strategy, any external training data, prior studies, or meta-analytic knowledge may be used — as long as it is declared in the registration form.
    Invited expert teams of up to two. Participation is by invitation, open to teams with a published track record in silicon-sample research (assessed through a short separate survey when you register your interest — not part of the registration itself). A team is at most two people; an individual may join any number of teams. We encourage invited teams to forward the call to other researchers they consider relevant.
    Multiple submissions are encouraged — up to two per tier. Enter more than once with genuinely different approaches (say, a per-respondent simulation and a direct forecast); each is a separate registered entry, capped at two per tier.
    Everything is registered — not everything must be public. The method form, the prediction files, and — strongly encouraged — code and raw model output logs are deposited on Zenodo (a free research archive run by CERN that gives each deposit a permanent DOI and a time-stamp — done through a release on a linked GitHub repository) before the lock date. Teams with proprietary methods may seal parts of their registration under the disclosure policy: locked and verifiable is mandatory, public is not.
  

  We deliberately welcome very different approaches. Submissions enter at one of three tiers, defined by the granularity of the prediction. The common currency across tiers is the set of preregistered estimands — the same quantities the human analysis produces — so every approach is scored on the rungs it can reach. Tier 1 is the preferred option: individual-level data flows through every analysis unchanged, so one Tier-1 entry is automatically scored on all of them — there is no need to also submit the same method at a lower tier. Otherwise, choose the highest tier your approach supports; lower tiers are simply eligible for fewer analyses.

  
    
      Tier 1 · Full
      Individual-level synthetic data
      A respondent-per-row dataset of behavioral clones: each synthetic participant completes the experiment in one condition. Flows through the preregistered analysis pipeline completely unchanged.
      Eligible for everything: ATE recovery, calibration regression, response distributions, subgroup heterogeneity, demographic baselines, and stereotyping diagnostics.
      Grain: 1 row = 1 synthetic respondent
    
    
      Tier 2 · Cell-level
      Group-level predictions
      A predicted mean per cell — per condition × outcome, and per condition × moderator-level × outcome — with a 95% uncertainty interval on the cell mean itself (the interval you believe has a 95% chance of containing the human cell mean; not the spread of individual responses). For approaches that simulate or reason about groups rather than individuals.
      Eligible for: ATEs, calibration, subgroup heterogeneity, and demographic baselines. Not eligible for distribution-shape metrics (OVL, KS) or predictability regressions.
      Grain: 1 row = 1 cell (condition × [moderator level ×] outcome)
    
    
      Tier 3 · Effect-level
      Direct effect predictions
      One predicted ATE per intervention × outcome, in original outcome units, with a 95% uncertainty interval on the ATE expressing the team's epistemic uncertainty. For zero-shot forecasting, in-context learning over the literature, meta-analytic priors, or hybrid pipelines.
      Eligible for: directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, RMSE, and calibration regression. Inferential agreement and TOST are computed from the submitted intervals.
      Grain: 1 row = 1 intervention × outcome estimate
    
  

  There is no fixed or required number of synthetic respondents per condition, and pool size never enters scoring. A larger pool only shrinks the Monte-Carlo noise in your own cell estimates, with diminishing returns once the per-cell mean is stable — so simulate enough that your predicted means are not sampling artifacts, but there is no benefit to matching the human N. For reference, each submission is scored against one half of the human sample (≈500 per intervention, ≈1,000 in control).

  
    Multiple entries welcome
    We actively encourage teams to enter more than once — but only for genuinely different approaches (say, a per-respondent simulation and a direct forecast), not to restack one method across tiers. Each approach is a separate entry with its own repository and registration; the team designates one as its primary entry and the rest as secondary entries. This label is purely the team's own ranking of which result to treat as headline: every entry — primary or secondary — is scored identically, on the same pipeline, and reported in the results table. Comparing a team's simulated-then-derived effects against its own direct forecasts isolates the value of the simulation step itself — one of the most interesting questions this benchmark can answer.
  

  Analysis eligibility by tier
  
  









Preregistered analysis
Tier 1
Tier 2
Tier 3




ATE recovery — six-metric ladder (Section 1)
Does the approach recover each intervention's average effect?
✓
✓
✓ *


Calibration regression α / β (Section 1)
Are predicted effects systematically biased or exaggerated?
✓
✓
✓


Response distributions — variance ratio, OVL, KS (Section 1)
Are the shapes of responses reproduced, not just means?
✓
—
—


Subgroup heterogeneity — condition × moderator (Section 2)
Do treatment effects vary across demographic groups as in humans?
✓
✓
—


Within-subgroup distributions (Section 2)
Are response shapes reproduced inside each demographic group?
✓
—
—


Demographic baseline calibration (Section 3)
Do subgroup means in the control condition match humans?
✓
✓
—


Demographic predictability / stereotyping (Section 3)
Do demographics predict responses with human-like strength, or do clones over-rely on them?
✓
—
—




  
  * For Tiers 2 and 3, the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals are scored directly — coverage (how often the human value falls inside) and an interval score that rewards honest, narrow intervals — and additionally supply the standard errors for inferential agreement and equivalence (TOST). For Tier 1, standard errors fall out of the fitted models, so no separate uncertainty input is required. Full metric definitions are in the Evaluation section and the benchmark preregistration.




  03Registration
  There are two separate steps, easy to confuse:
  
    Express interest — everyone, first. Every team fills the short Google interest form to leave contact details and confirm participation. This is not where you pick a tier or submit anything — it just puts you on the list and opens the eligibility check. Even invited teams and colleagues you forward the call to must complete it.
    Register your method — later, on Zenodo. The registration proper is the time-stamped Zenodo deposit described below, produced from the submission template repository. This is where the tier, the method description, and the predictions are locked.
  
  Before the lock, every team deposits one Zenodo record with two parts: a registration form describing the approach, and the prediction files it produced.
  The form. A structured form documents the pipeline in enough detail that, in principle, an independent team could re-run it and obtain a statistically equivalent submission; where exact replication is impossible (deprecated APIs, hardware nondeterminism), the deposited raw output logs become the verifiable record. It builds on the GUIDE-LLM reporting checklist1Feuerriegel, S., Barrie, C., Crockett, M. J., et al. (2026). A reporting checklist for large language models in behavioural science. Nature Human Behaviour, 1–5. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-026-02492-7 — the consensus reporting standard for LLM use in behavioral science — extended where a checklist alone is not enough for full pipeline replicability: exact models and call dates, prompts and survey-administration protocol, persona/profile construction, stochasticity and aggregation, post-processing, any learning or retrieval corpora, internal model and prompt selection, and frozen code plus raw output logs. The full item-by-item form is below; a fillable template (Markdown + machine-readable YAML) ships in the submission template repository.
  The predictions. Alongside the form you deposit the prediction file(s) for your tier, a machine-readable metadata.json, and — strongly encouraged — code and raw model output logs. Everything is hashed and time-stamped before the lock; the DOIs and SHA-256 hashes are emailed to the core team by the same deadline. Column schemas, file naming, the metadata.json format, and a self-check validator are on the materials page.
  The deposited, hashed record is final. Once you have emailed the DOI and fingerprints, that snapshot is your locked submission — it cannot be revised, and only the exact file matching the emailed SHA-256 hash is scored. You may keep working in your repository and even publish a newer Zenodo version afterwards, but the core team scores the version whose fingerprint you sent by the deadline. Deposit only when you are ready to lock.

  The registration form
  The form builds on the GUIDE-LLM reporting checklist and extends it where a reporting checklist alone is not sufficient for, in principle, full replicability of a prediction pipeline. Items concerning human participants or sensitive-data privacy (GUIDE-LLM D.1) are generally not applicable here, since every submission is fully synthetic; mark these N/A. When multiple models serve different pipeline stages, complete the model sections separately per model.

  Item codes follow the GUIDE-LLM checklist, extended where needed for full pipeline replicability. ★ marks items that must always be public — never escrowed or withheld; † marks items that must be at minimum escrowed — they may be sealed from the public, but never withheld from the core team (see the disclosure policy). Together the ★/† items form the non-waivable core that protects the validity of the benchmark. All other items may be escrowed freely, or withheld at the cost of having your entry classified as a sealed (Class C) entry.

  
  

Registration form — part 1 · Identity, scope, and system


Item
Name
What to report




0 · Approach identity and output


0.1 ★
Team
Team name, the one or two members (teams are at most two people), affiliations, and a corresponding contact.


0.2 ★
Plain-language summary
One paragraph describing the approach so that a non-specialist reader of the catalogue understands what was done.


0.3 ★
Submission tier & approach family
Tier (1 / 2 / 3) and approach family — e.g., one call per respondent vs. agent walking the full survey; single model vs. ensemble vs. multi-agent; simulation vs. direct forecasting; zero-shot vs. literature-conditioned.


0.4
Pipeline diagram
The full pipeline as an ordered sequence of steps (input → transformation → output), covering every stage from raw inputs to the submitted file. Prose descriptions of multi-stage pipelines are where replicability dies; a numbered step list or diagram is required.


0.5 ★
Coverage
Number of synthetic respondents / cells / estimates; mapping to conditions; coverage of the 16 interventions and 13 outcomes (all, or a declared subset with justification).


A · Scope of LLM use (GUIDE-LLM)


A.1
Purpose
Every workflow stage where LLMs are used — at minimum generating predictions; also persona construction, prompt optimization, post-processing, literature digestion, etc.


A.2 ★
Degree of automation
Confirmation that the pipeline is fully automated with no human in the loop at prediction time (a benchmark requirement); any exception and the oversight provided.


B · Model / system details (GUIDE-LLM, extended)


B.1
Model name(s)
Exact identifiers including provider, size, version/timestamp, and source link — e.g., gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 (OpenAI), Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (Meta, via HuggingFace). If several models were tested, name all and state which produced the submission and why (see also J.1).


B.2
Access & context mode
Access route (API / web / local), API name and version, chat vs. stateless calls, and exact call dates — providers update hosted models silently, so the date window is part of the model identity.


B.3
Configuration
All settings affecting output: temperature, top-p / top-k, max tokens, penalties, stop sequences, seeds, reasoning-effort settings, and number of completions per respondent / item.


B.4
Customization
Any adaptation beyond standard inference: fine-tuning (method, e.g., LoRA), RAG, automated prompt optimization, tool use, web search, agentic scaffolds, post-training. Cross-reference Section H for the data behind each.


B.5
Persistent memory
Whether sessions carried memory across interactions (yes / no / N/A), and if yes, what persisted.


B.6
Inference stack
For locally run models: serving framework and version (e.g., vLLM, llama.cpp, transformers), quantization (FP16 / INT8 / GGUF Q4 …), and hardware where nondeterminism matters. These change outputs and are invisible to GUIDE-LLM B.1–B.3 alone.


B.7
Ensembles
If multiple models or runs are combined: all members and the exact aggregation rule (averaging, voting, stacking, router logic).




  

  
  

Registration form — part 2 · Conditioning, administration, and stochasticity


Item
Name
What to report




C · Prompts (GUIDE-LLM)


C.1
Exact prompts
Verbatim prompt text(s) including any in-context examples, or a link to them in the deposited repository. State whether prompts were iteratively refined, and whether refinements were pre-specified or made in response to interim outputs.


C.2
System-wide instructions
Any system-level instructions guiding the model's general behavior.


C.3
Prompt-design rationale
A brief rationale for the prompt design: why prompts were structured as they were, and the reasoning behind any major design choices. Recommended, not required.


D · Persona / profile construction (Tiers 1–2)


D.1
Profile source
Source of demographic profiles you constructed: a public survey (e.g. GSS / ANES / Census), other survey, fully synthetic, or none. The benchmark ships no participant pool; report how you built yours, including how profiles were assigned to conditions.


D.2
Profile verbalization
Which profile variables are encoded and exactly how they are rendered into text — fixed template vs. LLM-generated persona narratives (if the latter: the generating model and prompt, recursively subject to this form).


D.3
Assignment & weighting
Number of personas, assignment to conditions (between- vs. within-subjects)—the team's responsibility, covering all 17 conditions—reuse across conditions, and any weighting or matching to a target population.


E · Stimulus and survey administration


E.1
Stimulus presentation
How interventions are shown to the model: verbatim text vs. paraphrase or summary; how state-contingent content (e.g., the extreme-weather intervention) is handled.


E.2
Survey walk-through
How items are administered: one item per call vs. blocks vs. whole survey; whether context carries across items; item and response-option ordering and any randomization; how response scales are displayed; how attention/comprehension elements are handled. The goal: enough detail to re-run the survey walk deterministically.


E.3
Response elicitation
How responses are obtained: free text, constrained choice, structured output, or token log-probabilities over the response scale (if logprobs: the normalization and mapping to scale values).


F · Stochasticity and aggregation


F.1
Runs & seeds
Number of runs per respondent / item / estimate; seeds where applicable; expected reproducibility of outputs under identical settings.


F.2
Aggregation rule
How multiple generations are consolidated into submitted values: mean, median, mode, first valid response, sampling one, distribution-preserving sampling. GUIDE-LLM asks for temperature; this item asks what you did with the variance — it directly shapes the distributional metrics.




  

  
  

Registration form — part 3 · Post-processing, learning components, blinding, and selection


Item
Name
What to report




G · Validation & post-processing (GUIDE-LLM, extended)


G.1
Human validation
Whether and how any human review of model outputs was performed (often N/A for fully automated generation; note any design-phase checks on pilot outputs).


G.2
Post-processing
Parsing rules from raw output to response scales; handling of refusals, malformed, missing, and out-of-range responses (re-prompt / drop / clip / impute); any exclusion criteria. For approaches that generate individual responses, also report the resulting effective N per condition (counts of valid generations behind each cell — this is descriptive disclosure, not a scoring input). Refusal handling alone can move distributions substantially — report counts.


G.3
Calibration corrections
Any post-hoc scaling, shifting, debiasing, or calibration applied to predictions — and exactly what data it was fit on (cross-reference H and I).


H · Learning and conditioning components


H.1
Fine-tuning data
For any fine-tuned component: the exact training corpus (with hashes or DOIs), hyperparameters, and checkpoint identifiers.


H.2
Context & retrieval corpora
For ICL / RAG / literature-conditioned approaches: the exact document set placed in context or indexed for retrieval, archived in the deposit. For direct-effect forecasters this corpus is the core of the method and must be fully enumerated.


I · Data inputs, blinding, and competing interests (GUIDE-LLM, extended)


I.1 ★
Competing interests
Funding, in-kind compute or model access, and professional or financial relationships with entities that have an interest in LLMs — particularly relevant where teams evaluate models built by affiliated organizations.


I.2 †
External human data
A self-declared list of all external human datasets that informed the approach anywhere (training, fine-tuning, retrieval, in-context examples, calibration anchors) — particularly published trust-in-science or climate-communication experiments.


I.3 ★
Blinding attestation
Mandatory. A signed attestation that no team member accessed, solicited, or was shown any human outcome data from this study, including pilots, before the prediction lock.


I.4 †
Contamination note
Training cutoff of every model used, relative to the public release dates of this project's preregistrations and Zenodo deposits — which are themselves potential contamination vectors for late-cutoff models. Note any known exposure of models to the project's materials.


J · Internal selection procedure


J.1 †
Design-space search
How the team arrived at the final pipeline: how many candidate configurations (models, prompts, hyperparameters) were tried; the internal validation criterion; and what data internal validation was run against. This is the researcher-degrees-of-freedom analog for simulation pipelines — invisible to GUIDE-LLM, but without it the benchmark partly measures luck in configuration lotteries rather than method quality.


K · Reproducibility & frozen artifacts (GUIDE-LLM F.1, extended)


K.1
Code & materials
Code, notebooks, prompts, and configuration shared (link / DOI), with secrets removed; determinism and seeds documented.


K.2 †
Raw output logs
Complete, unprocessed model responses archived alongside the processed submission, hashed and time-stamped in the Zenodo deposit. Code is necessary but insufficient: when a hosted model is deprecated, the logs are what remains verifiable. Required for Tiers 1–2 (public or escrowed — a withheld log makes the entry Class C); for Tier 3, required where intermediate generations exist. Logs too large for a GitHub release can be a separate Zenodo upload linked here.


K.3
Computational resources
API-call counts, total tokens, financial cost, and compute time — a GUIDE-LLM optional item made standard here, since cost-per-fidelity is itself a benchmark dimension.




  

  Disclosure policy — proprietary approaches
  Some teams — companies especially — cannot or will not publicly disclose their full approach. The benchmark separates two things usually conflated: verifiability (is the registration locked, time-stamped, and auditable?) and publicity (can everyone read it?). Verifiability is non-negotiable; publicity is graded. Each item is deposited as public (in the open Zenodo record), escrowed (in a restricted-access Zenodo record that still carries a DOI, a timestamp, and public SHA-256 content hashes — so the lock is verifiable — but is readable only by the core team and up to two independent auditors under a confidentiality agreement; an embargo with a sunset date, e.g. release upon publication or after 24 months, is encouraged), or withheld (not deposited at all, permitted only for items marked neither ★ nor † in the registration form above). An entry's disclosure class follows from its most restricted item:
  
  



Class
Definition
Consequences




Class A · Open
All registration items public.
Full results-table standing; entry is independently replicable in principle; all features enter the approach catalogue and the design-choice analysis.


Class B · Escrowed
Some items sealed, but every item is available to the core team and auditors under confidentiality.
Full results-table standing with an escrowed badge; compliance is verified but independent replication awaits the embargo sunset; only publicly disclosed features enter the design-choice analysis (the rest coded as undisclosed).


Class C · Sealed
One or more permitted items withheld even from escrow.
Scored and reported in the results table with a sealed / not independently verifiable flag; excluded from the approach catalogue and the design-choice analysis; the manuscript reports the entry as a performance claim that the core team could not audit beyond the always-public and escrowed-minimum items.




  
  
    How it works in practice
    Nothing is escrowed automatically. You set one disclosure choice per item — public, escrowed, or (only for items marked neither ★ nor †) withheld — as you fill in the registration form, and you deposit public items in the open Zenodo record and escrowed items in the restricted one. Your entry's overall disclosure class comes from your single most restricted item — you don't choose it separately. You write it into metadata.json as disclosure_class so it is machine-readable, but it has to match your item choices. So one withheld optional item makes the whole entry Class C; keep everything at least escrowed and it is Class B; make everything public and it is Class A.
  
  
    Why participate at all, if you can't disclose?
    For commercial teams, the value runs the other way: this is an adversarially designed, preregistered, third-party benchmark on genuinely unseen human data — a substantially stronger credibility signal than any internal evaluation, available at whichever disclosure class your IP constraints allow. The results table reports performance regardless of class; the badge tells readers exactly how much of the claim was auditable.
  





  04Evaluation
  
    The human–human baseline
    Scoring follows the preregistered analysis plan without modification. A preregistered random seed splits the human sample in half. Every submission is scored against Human 1. The agreement between Human 1 and Human 2 is the human–human baseline: how closely two independent halves of the human sample agree on the same estimands. This is a natural reference point for interpreting every method's scores, and it is reported in the same row format as every team.
  

  Every submission is scored against Human 1 on every metric and comparison its tier supports, across all 13 outcomes. The ATE-recovery ladder runs from least to most strict — knowing on which rung an approach breaks is more informative than any single pass/fail verdict. The scored effect for each outcome is a between-subjects post-treatment contrast — the intervention-group mean minus the control-group mean. There is no baseline adjustment; the survey's pre-treatment measures are not used in scoring.
  
  

Scoring metrics and comparisons. The ATE-recovery ladder is ordered from least to most strict; the further comparisons are computed wherever the submission's tier supports them.


Metric / comparison
What it asks




ATE-recovery ladder — increasing strictness, top to bottom


Directional agreement
Do you get the sign of each effect right? Chance = 50%.


Spearman ρ
Do you rank the interventions in the human order?


Pearson r
Are your effects proportional to human effects?


Inferential agreement
Would a researcher using your data reach the same significance conclusions?


RMSE
How large is the absolute magnitude error, in outcome units?


TOST equivalence
Is each predicted effect statistically equivalent to the human effect within ±50%?


Further comparisons — wherever the tier supports it


Calibration regression
Human ATEs regressed on predicted ATEs, separating additive bias (intercept α) from proportionality (slope β), with F-tests of β = 1 and of the joint α = 0, β = 1.


Subgroup effects
Recovery of condition × moderator interaction effects — whether treatment effects vary across the six demographic moderators as they do in humans, scored on the same six-rung ladder.


Distributional metrics (Tier 1)
Variance ratio, distribution overlap coefficient (OVL), and Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance — whether response shapes are reproduced, not just means; also within subgroups for the primary outcome.


Demographic baseline calibration
Pearson r and RMSE of subgroup means against humans in the control condition, computed per moderator.


Demographic predictability / stereotyping
R² from separate per-moderator regressions — whether demographics predict synthetic responses with the same strength as in humans, or whether clones over-rely on demographic cues.




  
  The exact estimands, metric definitions, and the full scoring pipeline — including how each metric above is computed and reported — are specified in the benchmark preregistration. It is the single authoritative source for how submissions are evaluated.
  
    Read the full scoring methodology & analyses →
  




  05Steps
  Participation is by invitation. Once you accept, you build your pipeline with any AI-based approach — any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, retrieval, or prior literature — using the intervention texts and the full survey instrument, and constructing your own participant profiles (the benchmark ships no pool), but never any human outcome data. You then register the approach and deposit your predictions before the lock. The roadmap marks which milestones are your team's responsibility (Your task) and which are the core team's (Core team).

  
    
      July 01, 2026 Core team
      Invitations & materials
      Invited teams receive the intervention texts, the survey instrument, the codebook, the registration template, and the validator — never a participant pool (teams build their own) and never any human outcome data.
    
    
      
        July 24, 2026 Your task
        Confirm intent
        Confirm participation by leaving your contact details in the interest form, then build your pipeline. Your tier is declared later, at registration.
      
      
        Participate
        Leave your contact details →
      
    
    
      July 30, 2026 Core team
      Human data collection completes
      The remaining human responses (toward N ≈ 18,000) are collected and cleaned, then held sealed — not shared, released, or analyzed until after the prediction lock.
    
    
      
        August 30, 2026 · Hard deadline Your task
        Submission deadline — register & lock
        Complete the registration form, generate your predictions, and deposit the form, prediction files, and logs on Zenodo — public or partly escrowed under the disclosure policy, hashed and time-stamped, with the hashes emailed by the same deadline. No revisions after.
      
      
        Register
        Submission template repo →
      
    
    
      September 30, 2026 Core team
      Scoring & first manuscript draft
      Every submission runs through the locked analysis pipeline unchanged; the results table and catalogue are assembled, and a first draft is shared with all co-authoring teams.
    
    
      October 31, 2026 Joint
      Pre-print & submission
      The joint manuscript is posted as a pre-print and submitted for publication, with all participating teams as co-authors.
    
  
  The human study was already partially fielded (≈16,000 of ≈18,000 responses) when the benchmark preregistration was drafted; none of these data have been shared, published, or made accessible to any prospective team.





  06Questions & contact
  Participation is by invitation; if you believe your team should be on the list, or you have questions about the registration form, the schemas, or edge cases of the rules, head to the contact page.
  
    Go to the contact page

title: Contact
text:
Participation in the Silicon Sample Benchmark is by invitation. If you believe your team should be on the list, or you have questions about the submission process, get in touch with the core team.
Reach us at janlukas.pfaender@gmail.com.
Clarifications of general interest are answered publicly on this website, so that all participating teams see the same information.

title: Submission template
text:
All working materials — the survey, the codebook, example submissions, the reporting checklist, and the scripts that clean and self-check a submission — live in a dedicated GitHub repository that is a submission template:
github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission

A home for your whole pipeline, not just a drop-box
Clone it (or click “Use this template”) and do your actual work inside it: your simulation code, prompts, profile construction, intermediate data — whatever your approach involves. The repository can hold as much or as little as you like. In the end we read just two things from it: your prediction data and your report (registration.md). Everything else is yours to organize. Full step-by-step instructions are in the repository’s README.


It does the tedious parts for you
Two pieces are why working inside the template is worth it:

an integrated cleaning script that turns raw Qualtrics-format output into the analysis-ready schema the locked analysis expects (composites, reverse-codes, age_band, …), so you don’t have to reconstruct it by hand;
a submission check that validates file structure, coverage, and value ranges before you deposit.

A random-placeholder example ships with the repo, so a fresh clone already passes the check — it shows you the exact target structure to aim for.


Blind what you can’t share
You can keep proprietary parts of your pipeline in the repository and simply gitignore them: your work stays in one place while only what you choose becomes public. This lets you respect the disclosure policy — locked and verifiable is mandatory, public is not — without splitting your project across repositories.
The submission file schemas and the human target sample (recruitment, census-based quotas, and the quota table) are specified in the benchmark preregistration.






Note



You build your own synthetic respondents. Generate them from any source — a public survey such as GSS/ANES/Census, fully synthetic personas, or none — and assign them to conditions yourself; declare your choice in registration item C.4. If you want to match the human target population, the benchmark preregistration’s census-based quota table is available to sample against.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Code
# packages
library(tidyverse)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
library(marginaleffects)
library(broom)
library(MetBrewer)
library(gghalves)
library(patchwork)
library(gt)
library(tinytable)
library(here)

# custom functions (reused unchanged from the original preregistration)
source(here("R/functions/statistics.R"))
source(here("R/functions/plots.R"))
source(here("R/functions/tables.R"))
source(here("R/functions/reporting.R"))

# simulated (random) placeholder data
human_data     <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/human_data_preregistration.rds"))
llm_data_teams <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/llm_data_preregistration_teams.rds"))

# Restrict the human data to the scored conditions (the 16 non-interactive
# interventions + control) BEFORE any model is fit. The real cleaned human
# data will additionally contain the three chatbot arms and the value-
# similarity arm, which no submission predicts; fitting the human models on
# those arms would put the human side's BH adjustment over a different test
# family than the submissions'. A no-op on the placeholder data (which has
# scored conditions only), load-bearing when the real loader is wired in.
scored_conditions <- levels(droplevels(factor(llm_data_teams$condition)))
human_data <- human_data |>
  filter(condition %in% scored_conditions) |>
  mutate(condition = droplevels(condition))

# Same preregistered random split of the human sample as in the original
# preregistration. Human 1 is the reference half all submissions are scored
# against; Human 2 is the human replication reference.
set.seed(42)
split_ids    <- sample(unique(human_data$id), size = floor(length(unique(human_data$id)) / 2))
human_data_1 <- human_data |> filter(id %in% split_ids)
human_data_2 <- human_data |> filter(!id %in% split_ids)

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
About this preregistration
Why a second preregistration. The project began as a single-team simulation study, preregistered as “Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs” (the original preregistration, v1). That plan asked how well a single approach, a group-level simulation built by the core team from demographically representative U.S. profiles with a particular model and prompt, recovers a novel megastudy. But a single team’s result is only one point in a large design space of ways to build a synthetic sample. This document reconceives the project as a multi-team benchmark: many independent teams predict the same novel experiment with any AI-based approach, and every approach is scored identically. It is now the canonical, self-contained plan for the project, superseding the original v1 preregistration.
What it keeps from v1. The scientific target and the analytical backbone are unchanged. The estimands (average treatment effects per intervention × outcome, subgroup interactions, control-condition response distributions, and demographic baselines), the three-section analysis structure, the least-to-most-strict metric ladder, and the preregistered split-half human replication reference are all carried over from v1 and restated in full below (see Preregistered analysis plan) rather than referenced by pointer. The model and comparison functions in R/functions/statistics.R are reused unchanged.
What it changes and adds. This preregistration is written for teams who are blinded to the human outcome data. Revising the analysis plan therefore does not carry the usual risk of data-contingent researcher degrees of freedom. The changes fall into three groups: how the study is reconceived, what it now reports, and a set of scoring add-ons drawn from the recent silicon-sample literature. Each is summarized here at a high level, with a pointer to the section that specifies it in full.
A. From one approach to a field. These change the design. They leave v1’s analysis pipeline untouched.

Single team → multi-team benchmark. The project now scores a field of independent submissions rather than one core-team approach, through three submission tiers defined by prediction granularity (Tier 1 individual-level synthetic data, Tier 2 cell-level statistics, Tier 3 effect-level estimates with uncertainty intervals). See Benchmark design.
Method registration and disclosure. Every team documents its pipeline on a standardized method-registration form (an extended GUIDE-LLM checklist) and deposits its predictions under a disclosure policy that keeps even proprietary entries verifiable. See Method registration and disclosure.
Per-tier submission specifications. Each tier has an exact file schema, and the shared estimands are recovered from whatever a tier supplies. For instance, effect standard errors are reconstructed from the uncertainty intervals that Tiers 2–3 submit. See Submission specification and Tier-specific estimand recovery.

B. What the benchmark reports. These sit on top of v1’s pipeline. No estimand, model, or metric inherited from v1 is redefined.

The headline result is the field, not a single scorecard. The primary result is no longer one approach’s report card but how the whole field of approaches fares at predicting human behavior. The main purpose is to present the distribution of predictive quality across the field. Every approach is scored against a split-half human replication reference (how well one half of the human sample predicts the other). Teams may enter more than once, so the field summary counts one primary entry per team. A team may also choose to predict only part of the study rather than every intervention and outcome. The main leaderboard is therefore limited to full-coverage entries (those that predicted the complete set), so its scores are all measured on the same basis. Partial entries are reported separately. See Cross-team comparison.
One score per metric, pooled across outcomes. A single data point is one predicted effect placed next to the matching human effect, for example how much a particular message shifted donations to the science organization. For each metric on the ladder, all such comparisons (every intervention paired with every outcome, each expressed in percentage points of its outcome’s scale range — see item 16) are pooled into one number, which uses all of them at once rather than scoring each outcome on its own. We deliberately do not collapse the metrics into one overall “champion” score. An approach can get the direction of effects right while mis-scaling their size, or do well on the main outcome and badly on the rest, and a single number would hide exactly the structure the benchmark exists to reveal. Each metric is therefore read on its own. See Cross-team comparison (Scoring principle).
An uncertainty interval on every score. The benchmark tests 16 interventions, but these are only a sample of the messages one could have tested. A different study could have used 16 others of the same kind. An approach’s score is therefore an estimate, and like any estimate it would move somewhat under a different draw of interventions. We attach an interval to every score showing how much it would move, so that a small gap between two approaches is not mistaken for a real difference when it sits within that wobble. For the same reason, we never call one approach better than another from the ranking alone: the claim has to pass a preregistered test that compares the two directly. See Uncertainty intervals on the leaderboard.

C. Evaluation add-ons from the silicon-sample literature. The remaining additions extend how an approach is scored, each motivated by how recent work validates synthetic samples. Every one is new to this preregistration, supplements rather than replaces an inherited metric, and points to the section where it is specified in full.

A noise-corrected correlation. The human effects an approach is scored against are themselves estimated from a finite (here, half-split) sample, so they carry sampling noise that mechanically drags every correlation down. This penalizes all approaches, and the reference itself, for something unrelated to predictive skill. Following Ashokkumar et al. (2026), we report a noise-corrected correlation beside the raw one (the raw value stays the ranked metric). See Comparison functions, and Section 2, where small demographic cells make the correction matter most.
Did it pick the right interventions? The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern, but the decision a many-arm megastudy exists to inform is narrower: which interventions are worth acting on. We score that directly: are an approach’s top-ranked interventions the ones with the largest true effects (Ashokkumar et al. 2026)? See Section 1.
A floor for the rungs without a natural zero. Some rungs (inferential agreement, equivalence, and RMSE) have no natural zero to read them against, and on inferential agreement a lazy “predict no effect for everything” strategy scores deceptively well when most true effects are small and not statistically significant. Adding a naive “do-nothing” approach as a floor reference makes these scores interpretable. Each approach is then read as the distance between that floor and the human reference (Ashokkumar et al. 2026; Cummins 2025). See Comparison functions.
A fixed bound for the equivalence test. One rung asks whether a predicted effect is close enough to the human effect to count as equivalent, which needs a bound for “close enough.” v1 set that bound as a fraction of the human effect being tested. But that effect is itself measured with error, so the bound moved with the noise and the test was not valid. We now fix the bound in advance, at a set fraction of each outcome’s scale, so it no longer depends on the numbers under test. See Comparison functions.
Is an approach consistent across metrics? Cummins’ central finding is that a configuration strong on one measure is routinely weak on another, so any single-metric verdict misleads. We report how stable each approach’s rank is across the whole ladder (Cummins 2025). See Cross-team comparison.
A fairness gap, and under-dispersion foregrounded. Averaging baseline accuracy over demographic groups can hide an approach that serves most groups well but consistently misses one, so we report the gap between the worst- and best-served group (Park et al. 2026). Because too-similar synthetic responses (under-dispersion) are the failure mode the literature flags most often, we make under-dispersion the headline distribution diagnostic. See Section 3, and Section 1 (response distributions).
Does the result depend on an arbitrary split? The human sample is divided in half at random to build the reference. That split is fixed before any data are seen, so it is not a researcher degree of freedom. But the particular split we drew is still arbitrary, and another random split would serve just as well. To show the results do not hinge on the one we happened to draw, we recompute them across many alternative splits and report how stable they are (Cummins 2025). See Cross-team comparison.
A stated scope: populations, not individuals. The benchmark cannot assess individual-level fidelity, because teams build their own synthetic respondents with no pairing to specific humans. Its distribution metrics are therefore claims about the population response shape, not about matching any person. We say so plainly, because aggregate agreement can coexist with wrong individual behavior (Park et al. 2026; Arruda et al. 2026). See Overview (scope statement).
Scoring the uncertainty intervals themselves. Teams in Tiers 2–3 submit not only a predicted effect but an uncertainty interval around it, and we use that interval to decide whether the effect counts as statistically significant (does the interval exclude zero?). This opens a loophole: a team that submits very wide intervals will have every interval include zero, so every prediction reads “not significant”. Since most effects in behavioral experiments are expected to be relatively small, matching “not significant” everywhere might earn a high agreement score with no real skill, just by being vague. To close the loophole we also score the intervals directly: whether they are well-calibrated (containing the true effect about as often as they claim to) and appropriately narrow. Being vague then carries its own cost. See Tier-specific estimand recovery.
One unit for all pooled scores. The outcomes sit on different scales: 0–100 attitude sliders, a $0–10 donation, a 0–1 signup probability. Pooling effects in their raw units would let the sliders drown out the two behavioral outcomes (whose effects are numerically 10–100 times smaller) and would hand every submission a cluster of free near-zero points. Every estimate is therefore converted to percentage points of its outcome’s scale range before any cross-outcome scoring, following the practice of Ashokkumar et al. (2026), who pool effects only in these units and never across raw scales. With one common unit, RMSE also becomes comparable across outcomes and is reported pooled (it previously could not be). The binary newsletter outcome is added to the subgroup analysis on the same footing, via the linear probability model. This changes preregistered scoring quantities, which is why it is made now, before any team registers. See Cross-team comparison (Scoring principle) and Section 2.

Everything in groups B and C is additive: each item adds to an inherited metric without removing or redefining one. Two things do change from v1, both only for the cross-team comparison and both explained above. First, the equivalence test now uses a bound fixed in advance (item 10). Second, we keep v1’s split-half human comparison exactly as it was and change only its name: v1 called it a “ceiling,” which wrongly implies the best any approach could reach, so we rename it the human replication reference (item 4).

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Motivation
Whether large language models can stand in for human participants is a consequential open question in behavioral science, and the evidence so far is mixed and hard to interpret. Most existing evaluations target experiments that were already published, and plausibly in the models’ training data. Almost none are preregistered, so reported successes are entangled with researcher degrees of freedom in model and prompt choice (Cummins 2025).
We therefore aim to provide a preregistered, multi-team prediction benchmark, the Silicon Sample Benchmark: independent teams predict the same novel megastudy (N ≈ 18,000) with any AI-based approach, and every approach is scored on the same preregistered metric ladder. The design addresses both problems: (i) the target is novel (none of the interventions has been tested in this form), and (ii) every prediction is deposited and time-stamped before any human data are released. The full rationale, rules, and incentives for participating teams are laid out in the call for participation.
The benchmark scores the 16 non-interactive text interventions of the megastudy1, across self-reported attitudes (e.g., trust in climate scientists, support for funding of climate science) and behavioral measures (a donation to a scientific organization, a newsletter sign-up).
Table 1 gives an overview of the 16 interventions, grouped by their content. All are short text messages (e.g., an article, an interview excerpt, corrected statistics, a portrait) shown once before the outcome battery. Together with the shared control condition they make up the 17 conditions every submission needs to cover.


Code
interventions <- read_csv(here("data/interventions.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

tag_order <- c(
  "Collaboration and peer-review",
  "Scientific methods and results",
  "Applications and impact",
  "Others' endorsement",
  "Values",
  "Other"
)

# Same table layout as v1: category headers inserted
# as bold full-width rows, with a running intervention number.
intervention_table_data <- interventions |>
  mutate(across(c(title, summary, tag, code_name), str_trim)) |>
  filter(tag != "LLM-chatbot", title != "Value similarity") |>
  mutate(tag = factor(tag, levels = tag_order)) |>
  arrange(tag, title) |>
  mutate(number = as.character(row_number())) |>
  # internal condition code names exactly as in the raw survey files
  # (survey.qsf / survey_definition.json); semicolon-joined names are a
  # single identifier — semicolons are part of the name, never split.
  mutate(codename = code_name) |>
  group_by(tag) |>
  group_modify(~ bind_rows(
    tibble(title = as.character(.y$tag), codename = "", summary = ""),
    .x
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(` ` = ifelse(title %in% levels(tag), "", number)) |>
  select(` `, title, codename, summary) |>
  rename(`Intervention Title` = title, `Code name` = codename, Summary = summary)

# identify header rows
header_rows <- which(intervention_table_data$` ` == "")

intervention_table_data |>
  tt(width = c(0.04, 0.19, 0.17, 0.60)) |>
  format_tt(escape = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(align = "l", alignv = "t") |>
  style_tt(i = 0, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(i = header_rows, bold = TRUE, j = 2, colspan = 3) |>
  style_tt(j = 3, monospace = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(fontsize = 0.85)




Table 1: The 16 text-based interventions scored in the benchmark, grouped by the broad angle each takes on trust in climate scientists. The Code name column gives the internal condition identifier used in the raw survey files (survey.qsf / survey_definition.json), for teams that parse those directly. Each intervention has exactly one code name; where intervention-author teams were combined, the code name is a semicolon-joined list of animal pairs, and the semicolons are part of the name — match on the full string, never split it (see survey/condition_codenames.csv in the benchmark repository). The original megastudy also fields three interactive LLM-chatbot arms and a ‘Value similarity’ quiz; these four are interactive and excluded from the simulation benchmark, leaving these 16 plus a shared control condition (17 conditions in total).





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                Intervention Title
                Code name
                Summary
              
        
        
        
                
                  
                  Collaboration and peer-review
                  
                  
                
                
                  1
                  Interview Prof. Maraun
                  flimsy fish
                  Climate scientist Prof. Douglas Maraun at the University of Graz in Austria stresses the collaborative and self-correcting process of climate science.
                
                
                  2
                  Peer-review
                  honored haddock
                  What makes climate science trustworthy is the process of independent peer-review.
                
                
                  
                  Scientific methods and results
                  
                  
                
                
                  3
                  Measurement & modeling (1)
                  perfect prawn
                  Climate scientists use sophisticated measurement and computational modeling techniques to surveil climate and predict how it changes.
                
                
                  4
                  Measurement & modeling (2)
                  orchid orangutan; defiant dragonfly
                  Climate scientists are primarily natural scientists (e.g., biologists, physicists). They use use sophisticated tools and quantiative methods to measure and predict climate change.
                
                
                  5
                  Model accuracy
                  apple aardvark
                  This is an edited version of a real news article showcasing that even old climate models, despite some flaws, were remarkably correct in predicting global warming.
                
                
                  
                  Applications and impact
                  
                  
                
                
                  6
                  Extreme weather predictions
                  practical planarian
                  Showcases how climate science predicts and helps adapting to different extreme weather events (blizzards, floods, wildfires). Takes into account a participant's state and adresses the extreme weather event most common in that state.
                
                
                  7
                  Portrait Prof. Cherry
                  complicated cockroach
                  Todd Cherry, a scientist focused on climate issues, is integrated in his local community, and does work that is relevant for this community.
                
                
                  
                  Others' endorsement
                  
                  
                
                
                  8
                  Corporate reliance
                  giant gibbon; brick bobcat
                  Insurance companies and large corporations rely on climate scientists' projections.
                
                
                  9
                  Former skeptics
                  limping llama; friendly frog
                  Former climate change skeptics Jennifer Rukavina (television meteorologist) and Bob Inglis (former Repubican congressman) explain how they came to change their mind.
                
                
                  
                  Values
                  
                  
                
                
                  10
                  Interview Prof. Sebille
                  heartfelt hummingbird
                  Prof. Erik van Sebille, a climate scientist and oceanographer at Utrecht University, Netherlands, mentions harmful consequences of climate change on oceans and humans, and how he cares about preventing these consequences.
                
                
                  
                  Other
                  
                  
                
                
                  11
                  Consensus
                  jealous jaguar
                  Correcting potential misperceptions on the level of agreement among climate scientists on cliamte change, and climate change related information.
                
                
                  12
                  Funding
                  phony parrotfish
                  Correcting potential misperceptions on the amount and sources of climate science funding. Showcases that climate science receives relatively little pubilc and private funding.
                
                
                  13
                  High public trust
                  crushing chicken; gross grasshopper; homely halibut
                  Correct potential misperceptions of how many Americans trusts climate scientists. A majority of Americans trusts climate scientists at least to some extent.
                
                
                  14
                  Oil industry misinformation
                  worse wildfowl
                  Oil companies have spent decades financing large propaganda campaigns to cast doubt on the existence climate change and the credibility of climate scientists.
                
                
                  15
                  Scientist community helpers
                  periwinkle partridge
                  Climate scientists are members of local communities and their work helps local communities in times of cliamte disasters (e.g., floods and wildfires).
                
                
                  16
                  Social justice
                  difficult dog
                  In the United States, the wealthiest 10% of the population are responsible for roughly 40% of the country’s total greenhouse gas emissions. Climate scientists provide evidence to hold the emitters accountable.
                
        
      
    






The first goal of the benchmark is not to crown a single champion team. It is to map the current design space of synthetic sample approaches and to evaluate, as a field, how well they predict human behavior. This benchmark study will provide a catalogue: a structured, comparable description of how research teams currently approach AI prediction of behavioral experiments (open- vs. closed-weight models, persona sources, administration protocols, fine-tuning, in-context learning) and which of these choices appear to matter. We will evaluate approaches on a ladder of metrics of increasing strictness, from directional agreement to formal equivalence, plus distributional fidelity and stereotyping diagnostics (all specified in full under Preregistered analysis plan, below). We expect different approaches to win on different rungs.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
The human target sample
Predictions are scored against the human data from the megastudy itself. Participants are recruited from a non-probability opt-in panel, quota-matched to the population of U.S. residents, all aged 18 or older, who pass a series of attention and bot-detection checks before treatment assignment.
The benchmark targets N ≈ 18,000 complete responses: 1,000 per intervention across the 16 text-based interventions, plus 2,000 in the shared control condition. Recruitment uses census-based cross quotas on gender × age and gender × race/ethnicity to approximate the U.S. adult population. The targets below match the proportions of the parent megastudy, with counts rescaled to a total N ≈ 18,000. Participants selecting “Other” as their gender are not quota-constrained.
The targets below are drawn from the 2024 U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates Program. Total is the share within each category. Male and Female give the within-category gender split. The table describes the key demographics of the human sample. Participating teams are not required to use this information for their own synthetic sample.


Code
# Same layout as the interventions table: category headers inserted as bold
# full-width rows above their levels (tinytable, for a consistent look).
quotas <- read_csv(here("data/quotas_18000.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

quota_table_data <- quotas |>
  group_by(Variable) |>
  group_modify(~ bind_rows(
    tibble(Category = as.character(.y$Variable), Total = "", Male = "", Female = ""),
    .x
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(Category, Total, Male, Female) |>
  rename(` ` = Category)

header_rows_q <- which(quota_table_data$Total == "")

quota_table_data |>
  tt(width = c(0.40, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20)) |>
  format_tt(escape = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(align = "l", alignv = "t") |>
  style_tt(i = 0, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(i = header_rows_q, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(fontsize = 0.85)




Table 2: Sampling quota targets (N ≈ 18,000), from the 2024 U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates Program. Proportions match the parent megastudy, with counts rescaled to N ≈ 18,000, while the target of the megastudy is N≈ 22,000.

The six demographic moderators used for the subgroup and calibration analyses (gender, age, race, education, income, and partisan identity) are specified under Preregistered analysis plan (below) and in the codebook. Full sampling and exclusion detail is in the human study preregistration.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Benchmark design
Participation is by invitation. Each team builds an AI-based prediction pipeline from the shipped materials (intervention texts, the full survey instrument, the codebook, the registration template, and the validator).
Teams can use any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, retrieval, or prior literature they want. They will receive no participant pool (each team builds its own profiles) and never any human outcome data. Before the deadline for the prediction lock, each team registers its approach by depositing its synthetic sample/predictions and materials on Zenodo, hashed and time-stamped. After the prediction lock deadline (and once the human data collection is finished), every submission runs through the preregistered analysis pipeline unchanged and is reported on a public leaderboard alongside the human replication reference, and in a joint manuscript with all participating teams as co-authors. Three ground rules define a valid entry and matter for the analysis. Blinding is absolute: no team accesses, solicits, or is shown any human outcome data before the lock (at registration time ~16,000 of the planned ~18,000 responses had been collected and none released). Pipelines are AI-based and automated. Everything is registered though not necessarily public (see the disclosure policy below). The full procedure, ground rules, timeline, and incentives are in the call for participation.
Organizer access and firewall. Blinding constrains the organizers too, so we state who has seen what. Interim human data (the ~16,000 responses collected at the time of writing) have been accessed only by the research lead, Jan Pfänder, for data-quality monitoring. As a result, members or affiliates of the core team who have had no access to any human outcome data may enter the benchmark as regular teams under the same rules as everyone else (same registration form, same blinding attestation, same deadlines).
For full disclosure, Jan Pfänder presented some very preliminary results (human megastudy sample at the time N ≈ 3000) from an initial simulation attempt (following preregistration v1) in a 5min talk at a closed academic workshop at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin (May 2026). This is the only presentation that has targeted an audience of potential collaborators on the benchmark study. It focused on the prediction ability of the simulation approach and revealed nothing about how humans behaved in the megastudy. Two other talks were explicitly about the human megastudy results: Jan Pfänder presented preliminary results from the human megastudy at an invited talk at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin (May 2026, N at the time ≈ 3000 of 22000), and at a small conference at the University of Hohenheim (June 2026, N at the time ≈ 13000 of 22000). No one in the audience of these talks will be invited to participate in the benchmark study. In addition, since we ask for extensive disclosure of materials, a “cheating” prompt (e.g., “make sure intervention XX does best”) would surface in the reporting framework that teams are required to fill out.

Three ways to compete
We deliberately welcome very different approaches. Submissions enter at one of three tiers, defined by the granularity of the prediction. The common currency across tiers is the set of preregistered estimands, the same quantities the human analysis produces, so every approach is scored on the rungs it can reach. Teams choose the highest tier their approach supports. Lower tiers are simply eligible for fewer analyses.

Tier 1 · Full: individual-level synthetic data. A respondent-per-row dataset of behavioral clones: each synthetic participant completes the experiment in one condition. Flows through the preregistered analysis pipeline completely unchanged. Eligible for everything: ATE recovery, calibration regression, response distributions, subgroup heterogeneity, demographic baselines, and stereotyping diagnostics. Grain: 1 row = 1 synthetic respondent.
Tier 2 · Cell-level: group-level predictions. A predicted mean and a 95% uncertainty interval on that mean per cell (per condition × outcome, and per condition × moderator-level × outcome). For approaches that simulate or reason about groups rather than individuals. Eligible for ATEs, calibration, subgroup heterogeneity, and demographic baselines. Not eligible for distribution-shape metrics (OVL, KS) or predictability regressions. Grain: 1 row = 1 cell (condition × [moderator level ×] outcome).
Tier 3 · Effect-level: direct effect predictions. One predicted ATE per intervention × outcome, in original outcome units, with a 95% uncertainty interval expressing the team’s epistemic uncertainty. For zero-shot forecasting, in-context learning over the literature, meta-analytic priors, or hybrid pipelines. Eligible for directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, RMSE, and calibration regression. Inferential agreement and TOST are computed from the submitted intervals. Grain: 1 row = 1 intervention × outcome estimate.



Analysis eligibility by tier









Preregistered analysis
Tier 1
Tier 2
Tier 3

✓
✓
✓ *

✓

✓

* For Tiers 2 and 3, inferential agreement and equivalence (TOST) are evaluated against the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals rather than model-based standard errors. For Tier 1, standard errors fall out of the fitted models, so no separate uncertainty input is required.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Method registration and disclosure
Before the prediction lock, every team documents its pipeline on a structured method-registration form and deposits it alongside the predictions. The form builds on the GUIDE-LLM reporting checklist (Feuerriegel et al. 2026), extended where a reporting checklist alone does not guarantee full replicability of a prediction pipeline. The full item-by-item form and the disclosure policy (which lets proprietary teams seal items under escrow while keeping the lock publicly verifiable) are published in the call for participation. A fillable template ships in the benchmark repository. Two aspects of the registration feed the analysis directly:

Approach characteristics recorded on the form (open- vs. closed-weight model, persona source, administration protocol, fine-tuning, in-context learning, and so on) are the features joined to the leaderboard for the exploratory design-choice analysis (see Cross-team comparison).
Disclosure class is a per-entry badge the policy assigns from the entry’s most restricted item: Class A · Open (all items public), Class B · Escrowed (some items sealed but available to the core team and auditors under confidentiality, so compliance stays verifiable), and Class C · Sealed (one or more items withheld even from escrow). All three classes receive full leaderboard standing, carrying their badge. Only publicly disclosed features enter the design-choice analysis, and Class C entries are excluded from that analysis only, not from the leaderboard.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Submission specification
Each tier defines the exact prediction file(s) a submission provides. The schemas below are the contract the scoring code consumes (build_pairs_t1() / build_pairs_t2() / build_pairs_t3(), see Cross-team comparison). The deposit logistics (Zenodo record structure, the metadata.json file, file naming, the self-check validator, and the public/escrowed split) are specified on the materials page and in the benchmark repository. Two requirements bear directly on the analysis: full coverage is all 16 interventions plus control across all 13 outcomes (declared subsets are permitted and scored pairwise, with coverage reported on the leaderboard), and condition and column labels must match the published codebook exactly (submissions are validated before the lock, and cells that fail to parse are dropped).

Tier 1 — individual-level dataset
One row per synthetic respondent, matching the structure of the cleaned analysis data (cleaned_llm.rds schema). Each team supplies its own synthetic respondents: profile_id is a team-assigned identifier, unique within the submission, and assignment of respondents to conditions (between-subjects, covering all 17 conditions) is the team’s responsibility, registered under C.4/C.6.








Column(s)
Type
Specification




profile_id
string
A unique respondent identifier you assign (unique within the submission).


condition
factor
Control or one of the 16 intervention labels, exactly as in the codebook.


gender, age_band, race, education, income, party
factor
The six moderators, levels per codebook (from your own profiles).


trust_multidimensional + 12 trust items
numeric
Primary composite 0–100. The 12 underlying items per codebook scale.


trust_post, distrust_post, funding_perceptions, policy_role_mean, inst_trust_mean
numeric
Secondary outcomes, original scales per codebook.


belief_post, concern_mean, policy_general, policy_specific_mean, behavior_mean
numeric
Tertiary outcomes, original scales per codebook.


donation_ams
numeric
Donation to AMS in $, per codebook.


newsletter_signup
0 / 1
Binary behavioral outcome.



Because a Tier 1 submission has the same shape as the human participant data, the same analysis code applies.
Precision requirement. An approach’s estimated effects are only as precise as the synthetic sample behind them. The fewer synthetic respondents, the noisier each estimated effect, and a very small sample would score poorly for a reason that has nothing to do with the method. A Tier 1 submission must therefore contain at least as many synthetic respondents as the human half it is scored against: 500 per intervention and 1,000 in the control condition. At that size the submission’s effects carry about the same sampling noise as the human reference, so neither side’s noise dominates the comparison.
We encourage teams to go well beyond this minimum. A larger synthetic sample gives more precise effect estimates that reflect the method itself rather than the luck of a particular draw, and synthetic respondents are cheap to generate. Ashokkumar et al. (2026) took the same approach, averaging many simulated responses per condition to keep their predictions precise.


Tier 2 — cell-level file(s)
Two long-format files. *_cells_main.csv: one row per condition × outcome. *_cells_moderator.csv: one row per condition × moderator × moderator-level × outcome.








Column
Type
Specification




condition
factor
Per codebook.


moderator, moderator_level
factor
Moderator file only. Levels per codebook.


outcome
string
Outcome variable name per codebook.


mean
numeric
Predicted cell mean on the original scale (proportion for newsletter_signup).


pi_lower, pi_upper
numeric
95% uncertainty interval bounds on the cell mean: the team’s epistemic uncertainty about the human cell mean, not a within-simulation SD. The ATE interval is derived by combining the treatment- and control-cell intervals. Used for inferential-agreement and equivalence scoring.





Tier 3 — effect-level file
One row per intervention × outcome (16 × 13 = 208 rows for full coverage). Estimates are ATEs versus control on the original outcome scale. The binary outcome is on the probability scale.








Column
Type
Specification




condition
factor
Intervention label per codebook.


outcome
string
Outcome variable name per codebook.


ate
numeric
Predicted ATE, original units.


pi_lower, pi_upper
numeric
95% uncertainty interval bounds: the team’s epistemic uncertainty about the human ATE, not a within-simulation standard error. Used for inferential-agreement and equivalence scoring.

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Preregistered analysis plan

Overview and the human replication reference
Each submission is scored against the human data with the same three-section plan, restated in full in the subsections below so that this document stands alone. The plan is applied symmetrically to the human sample and to every submission: the same model and comparison functions (R/functions/statistics.R) run on both, so a submission is never held to a standard the human data is not.
The human sample is split into two halves by a preregistered random seed. Human 1 is the reference half every submission is scored against. The agreement between Human 1 and Human 2 defines the human replication reference: how closely two independent halves of the human sample agree on the same estimands. Because every Tier 1 submission uses at least the same per-cell sample size as a human half (see Submission specification), this is the natural point of comparison. It shows how well a fresh sample of this size reproduces the reference half, which is the target a strong silicon sample aims to match. We renamed it from v1’s “ceiling,” which wrongly suggested the best any approach could reach. It simply measures how well two independent human halves of this size agree. The reference is computed with the identical code path and reported in the same row format as every team. It is the one fixed reference row in every results table. A floor baseline (“no effect”) anchors the other end for the metrics without a natural null (see Cross-team comparison). Because the split seed is itself an analytic choice, its influence is checked and reported: the reference (and, with real data, the full field) is recomputed across many alternative half-splits, so that results do not hinge on the seed we happened to draw (Robustness of the scoring pipeline, below). Every submission is scored under identical rules and appears on the leaderboard like any other. No entry receives a special reference row beyond the reference and floor.
Scope: aggregate and distributional fidelity, not individual fidelity. The benchmark tests whether an approach reproduces the megastudy’s estimands (treatment effects, subgroup patterns, and population response distributions), not whether any synthetic respondent matches any real person. It cannot test the latter by design: teams build their own synthetic respondents, with no pairing between a synthetic and a human individual, so an individual-level claim is never made or scored. The distinction matters beyond bookkeeping. Recent work shows that aggregate agreement can coexist with divergent individual-level behaviour: simulated populations that reproduce collective outcomes while getting individual heterogeneity and decision rules wrong [a “macro–micro dissociation”; Arruda et al. (2026)]. Benchmarks that do score individual fidelity require paired designs with per-person consistency ceilings (Park et al. 2026). Our distribution metrics (variance ratio, OVL, KS) are the population-level response to that warning. They catch a synthetic crowd whose members answer too much alike, but they should not be read as evidence about individuals.
Which of the analyses below applies to a given submission follows from its tier (see Analysis eligibility by tier): Tier 1 is eligible for all of them, Tiers 2–3 for the estimate-based subset.


Estimation functions
Every effect size compared below is produced by the same model functions, applied in the same way to the human reference and to each submission: average treatment effects per intervention for Section 1, and condition × moderator interactions for Section 2. These functions are unchanged from v1, and their full statistical rationale is in the human study preregistration. We reproduce them here so this document stands alone.
run_main_treatment_model() estimates each intervention’s ATE against the shared control by OLS with HC2 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors and BH-adjusted p-values.

Code
print_a_function_from_file("run_main_treatment_model")

run_main_treatment_model <- function(data,
                                     outcome,
                                     condition_var = "condition",
                                     covariates    = NULL,
                                     weights       = NULL,
                                     adjust_method = "BH") {
  
  # Formula
  rhs           <- paste(c(condition_var, covariates), collapse = " + ")
  model_formula <- as.formula(paste(outcome, "~", rhs))
  
  # Baseline (control) level
  baseline <- levels(data[[condition_var]])[1]
  
  # Fit
  fit <- lm(
    model_formula,
    data    = data,
    weights = if (!is.null(weights)) data[[weights]] else NULL
  )
  
  # Robust VCOV (HC2)
  vcov_robust <- sandwich::vcovHC(fit, type = "HC2")
  
  results <- lmtest::coeftest(fit, vcov = vcov_robust) |>
    broom::tidy(conf.int = TRUE) |>
    filter(str_detect(term, paste0("^", condition_var))) |>
    mutate(
      outcome          = outcome,
