# so it competes rather than floors; its interpretive content survives as the
# RMSE scale reference, the SD of the human ATEs.)
floor_pairs <- list(
  "Floor: no effect" = human_side |>
    mutate(estimate_l = 0, se_l = 0, infer_l = "ns") |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
)
floor_labels <- names(floor_pairs)

# Calibration is undefined for the floor (a constant prediction within the
# primary outcome -> zero-variance regressor), so their rows carry NA there.
# Its directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign, an artifact
# of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill (footnoted in the table).
floor_rows <- imap_dfr(floor_pairs, function(pr, sub) {
  pooled_metrics(pr, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp, include_rmse = TRUE) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, tier = "—", disclosure = "—",
           beta = NA_real_, calib_r2 = NA_real_,
           pi_coverage_pct = NA_real_, interval_score = NA_real_)
})

leaderboard <- bind_rows(
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    m <- submission_meta |> filter(submission == sub)
    leaderboard_row(pr, sub, tier = m$tier, disclosure = m$disclosure)
  }),
  floor_rows
) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(pearson_r))




Code
# Display labels / panel order for the metric ladder, and the null reference
# line per metric (50 = chance for directional agreement, 0 for the
# correlations; omitted for the percentage-agreement metrics where no clean
# null exists).
ladder_labels <- c(
  directional_pct = "Directional %",
  spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
  pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
  pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
  inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
  rmse            = "RMSE (pp)",
  rmse_adj        = "RMSE (adj., pp)",
  tost_pct        = "TOST %"
)
ladder_nulls <- c("Directional %" = 50, "Spearman ρ" = 0, "Pearson r" = 0,
                  "Pearson r (adj.)" = 0)

relabel_metrics <- function(df, labels) {
  df |> mutate(metric = factor(labels[metric], levels = labels))
}




Finding 1 — How well can the field predict the experiment?
The headline question is not who wins but how well the current generation of approaches can predict a novel experiment at all. To keep that readable, each question gets one key metric in the figures: Pearson r for ATE recovery, Pearson r on the interaction estimates for subgroup recovery, and the variance ratio for response shape. The correlation is the literature’s common currency (Ashokkumar et al. 2026), and one shared axis across the ATE and subgroup sections makes them directly comparable. The variance ratio carries the field’s one directional expectation (under-dispersion). The full preregistered ladders are computed for everything and reported in the companion tables.
Figure 1 shows the headline: the raw prediction task with the field’s calibration lines and coefficients (A), and the field and its ranking on the pooled Pearson r (B). One rendering note on panel B: this placeholder demonstration runs the bootstrap with a small replicate count (n_boot_demo = 200) to keep the document cheap to build. The preregistered value for the real-data run is n_boot_real = 2,000. When the submissions arrive, only that parameter changes, nothing else about the procedure.


Code
# (A) the raw prediction task: all approaches, all outcomes. The pairs are
# already in pp of scale range (pp_scale_pairs() at pair-building), the same
# unit as every pooled metric.
headline_pairs <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y))
p_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  headline_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted ATE (pp of scale range)",
  y_lab       = "Human ATE (pp of scale range)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.15, point_size = 0.5,
  ref_alpha   = 0.6,  ref_size   = 1,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Average field / human calibration lines + a corner readout of correlation
# (r, adj.) and coefficients (β, α with CI). Mean field Pearson r and its
# noise-corrected value come from the pooled leaderboard — the identical pp
# quantity ranked in panel B; the coefficients are fit from the same pp pairs
# by calibration_overlay().
field_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(!submission %in% floor_labels,
                                  submission != "Human replication")
human_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_scatter <- p_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(headline_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = human_ld$pearson_adj)

# (B) ranked field on the key metric, with cluster-bootstrap intervals.
# n_boot_demo here; the real-data run uses n_boot_real = 2,000 (see variables).
pooled_f <- \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp)
ate_forest <- cached("amendment_ate_forest", {
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    cluster_boot(pr, pooled_f, cluster = "condition", n_boot = n_boot_demo) |>
      mutate(submission = sub)
  }) |>
    relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)
})
p_forest <- plot_field_forest_single(ate_forest, tag = "B")

(p_scatter | p_forest) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.15))








Figure 1: Headline cross-team figure (placeholder data). (A) Predicted against human ATEs across all outcomes and approaches, in percentage points of each outcome’s scale range (as in the predicted-vs-observed figure of Ashokkumar et al.; the 0–100 sliders are already in pp, the behavioral outcomes rescale) — one point per approach × intervention × outcome, axes centred on zero, the dashed line the identity, red crosses the human replication reference (Human 2 predicting Human 1). The solid lines are the average calibration regression of human on predicted, pooled across all outcomes: dark blue the field (per-approach fits averaged), red the human replication reference. The corner prints the mean field Pearson r (and its noise-corrected value) and the human replication’s — the same pooled metric ranked in B — together with the field and human calibration coefficients (slope β, perfect = 1; intercept α, perfect = 0) with 95% CIs. (B) Approaches ranked by Pearson r pooled across all outcomes against Human 1: filled points with 95% cluster-bootstrap intervals over interventions, open points the noise-corrected r (adj.) where its guard allows a value, the human replication reference pinned on top (its value dropped as a dotted line). The bottom row summarises the field meta-analytically: the density of the approaches’ scores, the diamond their mean with its 95% CI, and the open point the mean noise-corrected r. On placeholder data every approach is a noise resample of the same dataset, so the field clusters near r = 0 in panel B — no relationship to the truth, as expected of a random resample.

Selecting the best interventions
The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern. A decision-maker’s question is narrower: if I trust this approach and act on its top picks, do I get the interventions that truly work best? topk_selection() scores exactly that (see Section 1): per outcome, each approach’s top k = 3 interventions by predicted ATE (≈ the top 20% of 16 arms, in the spirit of the intervention-selection use case that motivates Ashokkumar et al. (2026)) are compared with the truly best three by the human reference, via the hit rate (share of picks that are in the true best set, chance = 3/16 ≈ 0.19) and the regret ratio (mean true ATE of the picked set as a share of the best achievable, where 1 = perfect pick). The table shows both on the primary outcome and averaged across the continuous outcomes.


Code
topk_table <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  scores <- topk_selection(pr, k = 3)
  bind_cols(
    scores |>
      filter(outcome == outcomes_primary) |>
      transmute(hit_primary = hit_rate, regret_primary = regret_ratio),
    scores |>
      summarise(hit_mean    = mean(hit_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
                regret_mean = mean(regret_ratio, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, .before = 1)
}) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(hit_mean))

topk_table |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(hit_primary, regret_primary, hit_mean, regret_mean),
             decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission     = "Submission",
    hit_primary    = "Hit rate (primary)",
    regret_primary = "Regret ratio (primary)",
    hit_mean       = "Hit rate (mean)",
    regret_mean    = "Regret ratio (mean)"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  )

Where the field predicts well
Split by outcome (one distribution per outcome, scored across the interventions) and by intervention (one per intervention, scored across the 0–100 continuous outcomes — the pp conversion removes the unit mix, but a within-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes would still mix attitude and behavioral constructs, so the conservative subset is kept), the key metric shows where the field predicts well and what resists prediction (Figure 2). The full ladders per outcome and per intervention are in Table 5 and Table 6.


Code
# include_rmse: with all pairs in pp, RMSE and its noise-corrected companion
# RMSE_adj are defined per outcome and pooled alike; here they localise where
# the absolute error concentrates.
field_by_outcome <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr, "outcome",
                   f = \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp,
                                           include_rmse = TRUE)) |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, rmse, rmse_adj, tost_pct),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)

# Restricted to the 0-100 continuous outcomes: with pp units the scale
# confound is gone, but a per-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes
# would still mix constructs (attitude sliders vs. behaviors), so the
# conservative restriction is kept.
field_by_intervention <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr |> filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous), "condition") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, tost_pct),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)

# Companion-table helper: field median per metric within each group level,
# with the human replication reference value in parentheses.
field_group_table <- function(field_long, group,
                              pct_metrics = c("Directional %", "Inferential %",
                                              "TOST %")) {
  fmt <- function(v, pct) if_else(is.na(v), "—",
                                  if_else(pct, sprintf("%.0f", v),
                                          sprintf("%.2f", v)))
  med <- field_long |>
    filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
    group_by(across(all_of(group)), metric) |>
    summarise(med = median(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  ref <- field_long |>
    filter(submission == "Human replication") |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, ref = value)
  med |>
    left_join(ref, by = c(group, "metric")) |>
    mutate(pct  = metric %in% pct_metrics,
           cell = paste0(fmt(med, pct), " (", fmt(ref, pct), ")")) |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, cell) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = cell)
}

Subgroup recovery
Whether an approach reproduces who an intervention moves, not just its average effect. For each Tier-1 submission we fit the preregistered condition × moderator interaction model (run_moderator_model()) across the illustrative outcomes plus the binary newsletter signup, over the six demographic moderators, then pool the interaction estimates against Human 1 (compute_predicted = FALSE skips the marginal-effects step, which the leaderboard does not use). For the binary outcome the same OLS fit is a linear probability model, whose saturated interaction coefficient equals the difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions — the interaction on the probability scale (see the code note below). All interaction estimates are placed in percentage points of each outcome’s scale range before pooling, the same unit as Section 1. Because interaction estimates require respondent-level data, this view covers Tier 1 only. As specified in Section 2 above, the signed-effect rungs are scored (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r) plus the adjusted correlation radj, emphasized here because small subgroup cells make the reference noise non-uniform across cells (see Adjusted correlation and RMSE).


Code
# Human-1 interaction estimates, computed once and reused for every submission.
# The cached() keys keep their historical "amendment_" prefix (the analysis is
# unchanged); they are internal identifiers for analysis/results/*.rds, not tied
# to this file's name, so renaming the document does not require a recompute.
# The binary newsletter outcome runs through the same OLS machinery as a
# linear probability model: in the saturated condition × moderator
# specification the interaction coefficient is exactly the
# difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions — the interaction on
# the probability scale — and the HC2 robust SEs are valid for a binary
# response. It then pp-rescales (× 100) like every other outcome.
human_mod_side <- cached("amendment_human_mod_side", {
  map_dfr(outcomes_subgroup, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(human_data_1, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        # se_h feeds the adjusted correlation; only the human reference side
        # is corrected (see "Adjusted correlation and RMSE").
        select(condition, moderator_level,
               estimate_h = estimate, se_h = std.error) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
})

build_subgroup_pairs <- function(pred_data) {
  pred_side <- map_dfr(outcomes_subgroup, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(pred_data, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        select(condition, moderator_level, estimate_l = estimate) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
  inner_join(human_mod_side, pred_side,
             by = c("condition", "moderator_level", "outcome", "moderator")) |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
}

# Tier-1 submissions plus the reference are eligible. Interaction pairs are built
# once per submission and reused by the subgroup figure and its companion table.
subgroup_inputs <- c(
  set_names(
    map(tier1_ids, ~ llm_data_teams |> filter(team == paste0("team_", .x))),
    paste0("Team ", tier1_ids)
  ),
  list("Human replication" = human_data_2)
)
subgroup_pairs_list <- cached("amendment_subgroup_pairs", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, build_subgroup_pairs)
})


Figure 3 shows the moderation analogue of the headline figure: the raw prediction task on the interaction estimates (A), and the key metric per moderator (B), showing which kinds of heterogeneity the field can predict at all. The full signed-effect ladder, pooled, is in Table 7.


Code
moderator_label_map <- c(gender = "Gender", age_band = "Age",
                         race = "Race/ethnicity", education = "Education",
                         income = "Income", party = "Party ID")

# Pairs are already in pp of scale range (pp_scale_pairs() in the builder).
subgroup_scatter_pairs <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                                   ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y))
# ~37,000 pooled pairs: much lighter marks than the ATE scatter, or it reads
# as a solid blob.
p_sub_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  subgroup_scatter_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted interaction estimate (pp)",
  y_lab       = "Human interaction estimate (pp)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.05, point_size = 0.3,
  ref_alpha   = 0.2,  ref_size   = 0.5,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Mean field / human Pearson r on the interaction estimates (from the pooled
# signed-effect ladder, matching panel B), and the calibration lines + readout.
subgroup_ld <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                        ~ signed_metrics(.x) |> mutate(submission = .y))
sub_field_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission != "Human replication")
sub_human_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_sub_scatter <- p_sub_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(subgroup_scatter_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = sub_human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = sub_human_ld$pearson_adj)

field_subgroup_by_mod <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  pr |>
    group_by(moderator) |>
    group_modify(~ signed_metrics(.x)) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  transmute(submission,
            value      = pearson_r,
            moderator  = factor(unname(moderator_label_map[moderator]),
                                levels = unname(moderator_label_map)),
            metric     = factor("Moderators"))
p_sub_field <- plot_metric_distribution(
  field_subgroup_by_mod, x = "moderator", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Moderators" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "B", y = "Pearson r")

(p_sub_scatter | p_sub_field) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.3))








Figure 3: Subgroup recovery (placeholder data, Tier 1). (A) Predicted against human condition × moderator interaction estimates pooled across all approaches and the Section 2 outcome set (the four illustrative outcomes plus the binary newsletter signup), in percentage points of each outcome’s scale range, axes centred on zero; dashed identity line, red crosses the human replication reference. The solid lines are the average calibration regression of human on predicted (dark blue the field, red the human replication), with a corner readout of the mean field and human Pearson r (and its noise-corrected value) and calibration coefficients (β, α with 95% CI). (B) Distribution of each approach’s Pearson r on the interaction estimates, per moderator (pooled across the Section 2 outcomes), with the field mean and its 95% CI (white diamond) per row; red crosses the reference, dotted line the null.

Response-shape recovery
How well an approach reproduces the shape of the human response distribution, not only its mean. Using the preregistered compare_distributions() in the shared control condition, we score the overlap coefficient (OVL, 1 = identical densities), the Wasserstein-1 distance (W1, 0 = identical, computed as the integral of the absolute ECDF difference, in scale points), the Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance (KS, 0 = indistinguishable), and the variance ratio (clone/human, 1 = matched spread) for each continuous outcome. W1 is the field’s current standard distributional metric for bounded ordinal scales and is added here as the bandwidth-free companion to OVL. The KDE-based OVL depends on kernel bandwidths, which scale with the two samples’ sizes (a large synthetic sample is smoothed differently from the human half) and leak density past the 0/100 scale bounds. W1 has no tuning parameter and reads directly in scale points. The variance ratio stays the headline diagnostic (directional expectation: under-dispersion, ratio < 1). These are inherently per-outcome, control-condition quantities. Figure 4 therefore shows the raw response densities for every continuous outcome, one facet per outcome. Each facet stamps the field’s median variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance next to the human replication’s, so spread (VR) and overall distributional distance (W1) can be read at a glance for both the synthetic field and the human reference. All four metrics per outcome are in Table 8.


Code
dist_per_outcome <- function(pred_data) {
  map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
    compare_distributions(human_data_1, pred_data, out, control_lab,
                          include_w1 = TRUE) |>
      mutate(outcome = out)
  })
}

dist_labels <- c(ovl = "Overlap (OVL)", w1 = "Wasserstein-1",
                 ks_d = "KS distance", variance_ratio = "Variance ratio")

# Per-outcome shape metrics built once per submission, reused by the
# distribution figure and its companion table (cached: 29 x 11 KDE/ECDF
# passes over full respondent-level data).
dist_per_outcome_list <- cached("amendment_dist_per_outcome", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, dist_per_outcome)
})




Code
# Per facet, stamp the field's median variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance
# alongside the human replication's (Human 2 vs Human 1), so each panel shows
# both spread (VR) and overall distributional distance (W1) for the synthetic
# field and the human reference.
field_dist_ann <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list,
                           ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y)) |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
  group_by(outcome) |>
  summarise(vr = median(variance_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
            w1 = median(w1, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

human_dist_ann <- dist_per_outcome_list[["Human replication"]] |>
  select(outcome, w1, vr = variance_ratio)

dist_ann <- left_join(field_dist_ann, human_dist_ann,
                      by = "outcome", suffix = c("_field", "_human")) |>
  mutate(label = sprintf("Field   VR %.2f   W1 %.2f\nHuman  VR %.2f   W1 %.2f",
                         vr_field, w1_field, vr_human, w1_human)) |>
  select(outcome, label)

# wrapped primary-outcome label: the full one clips its facet strip
density_labels <- outcome_label_map
density_labels["trust_multidimensional"] <- "Multidimensional trust\n(primary)"

plot_density_overlay(
  human_data_1, human_data_2, llm_data_teams,
  outcomes       = outcomes_continuous,
  condition_val  = control_lab,
  outcome_labels = density_labels,
  annotations    = dist_ann,
  x_lab          = "Response (0–100, control condition)"
)








Figure 4: Response-shape recovery (placeholder data, Tier 1): control-condition response densities for every continuous outcome. In each facet, every Tier-1 approach is a thin line, Human 1 (the reference half) the bold line, and Human 2 (the replication half) the dashed red line. The corner stamp reports two numbers for that outcome, the variance ratio (VR, approach ÷ human, 1 = matched spread, below 1 = under-dispersion) and the Wasserstein-1 distance (W1, in scale points, 0 = identical distributions, larger = more different), for both the synthetic field (median across approaches, ‘Field’) and the human replication (‘Human’, Human 2 vs Human 1). VR captures spread, W1 captures how far the whole distribution sits from the human one in both location and shape. The full shape metrics per outcome are in the companion table.







Code
field_distribution <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list, function(d, sub) {
  d |>
    pivot_longer(c(ovl, w1, ks_d, variance_ratio),
                 names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  relabel_metrics(dist_labels)

field_group_table(field_distribution, "outcome", pct_metrics = character()) |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(outcome = "Outcome")

Finding 2 — Ranking approaches (secondary)
Having characterised the field, we rank individual approaches. This is the secondary, structural read: each metric is its own ranking, and the interesting result is which approaches lead on which rung. The leaderboard table is the main summary. The ranking visual for the key metric, with its cluster-bootstrap uncertainty intervals, is panel B of Figure 1.


Code
leaderboard |>
  select(submission, tier, disclosure, directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r,
         pearson_adj, pearson_fz, inferential_pct, rmse, rmse_adj, tost_pct,
         beta, calib_r2, pi_coverage_pct, interval_score) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj, pearson_fz,
                         rmse, rmse_adj, beta, calib_r2, interval_score),
             decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(directional_pct, inferential_pct, tost_pct,
                         pi_coverage_pct), decimals = 0) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission      = "Submission",
    tier            = "Tier",
    disclosure      = "Class",
    directional_pct = "Directional %",
    spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
    pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
    pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
    pearson_fz      = "Pearson r (per-outcome)",
    inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
    rmse            = "RMSE (pp)",
    rmse_adj        = "RMSE (adj., pp)",
    tost_pct        = "TOST %",
    beta            = "Calib. β",
    calib_r2        = "Calib. R²",
    pi_coverage_pct = "PI coverage %",
    interval_score  = "Interval score"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations. Divergence from the pooled Pearson r flags a submission that predicts which outcomes move without ranking the interventions within them.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pearson_fz)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Tiers 2–3 only: coverage of the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals (honest intervals land near 95) and the mean Winkler interval score on the 0–100 outcomes (lower = better; a proper score, so neither padding intervals wide nor squeezing them tight pays).",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pi_coverage_pct)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "The floor's directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign — an artifact of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill.",
    locations = cells_body(columns = directional_pct,
                           rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f4f4f4"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  )

Cross-metric consistency
A single-metric verdict can mislead: an approach that leads on one rung may trail on another, and Cummins (2025) show empirically that performance on one data feature routinely fails to transfer to others. Rather than leave that to a reader’s cross-panel comparison, we report it directly: the Spearman correlation between the approaches’ rankings on each pair of ladder rungs (reference and floor rows excluded). High values mean the rungs sort the field similarly, so a summary rank would be defensible. Low or negative values mean each rung tells its own story and no single number should be trusted alone.


Code
metric_cols <- names(ladder_labels)

rank_consistency <- leaderboard |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication", !submission %in% floor_labels) |>
  select(all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  cor(method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

rank_consistency |>
  as_tibble(rownames = "metric") |>
  mutate(metric = unname(ladder_labels[metric])) |>
  rename_with(~ unname(ladder_labels[.x]), all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -metric, decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "")

Design analysis: what the metrics can resolve at this sample size
Before locking the metric ladder, we check whether each rung has dynamic range at this design: whether a genuinely good submission can score visibly better than the floor. The check uses design constants and literature priors only and touches no data. The inputs are the design’s cell sizes (≈500 per intervention and ≈1,000 in control per human half), a typical 0–100 slider SD of ≈25 points, and plausible spreads of true megastudy ATEs (SDs of roughly 0.5–2 points: large message-based megastudies rarely move attitude scales by more). Three conclusions follow. First, the reference half-sample ATE SE is ≈1.4 points, the noise floor under every raw metric. Second, we can compute the expected human-replication correlation and the expected correlation of a perfect, noiseless predictor for each plausible true-ATE spread. The perfect predictor’s is the square root of the replication row’s. When the true spread is small relative to 1.4, the radj NA-guard is expected to bind for many entries. Third, v1’s relative TOST bound Δ = 0.5 × |ATEh| (≈0.25–1 point) is smaller than the smallest difference the test could ever confirm (1.645 × sediff ≈ 2.3 points even for a noiseless submission), so that rung would read ~0% for every entry including the human replication row. That is a dead metric. The preregistered fixed bound of 3 scale points (3% of range) gives a perfect predictor roughly 40% per-pair equivalence power while remaining unreachable for badly wrong predictions: dynamic range restored, at the cost of a laxer notion of equivalence, which we accept and state.


Code
sd_y     <- 25
n_arm_h  <- 500
n_ctrl_h <- 1000
se_ate_h <- sd_y * sqrt(1 / n_arm_h + 1 / n_ctrl_h)
z05      <- qnorm(0.95)

tost_power <- function(delta, se) {
  margin <- delta - z05 * se
  if (margin <= 0) 0 else pnorm(margin / se) - pnorm(-margin / se)
}

tibble(tau = c(0.5, 1, 2)) |>
  mutate(
    se_reference    = se_ate_h,
    r_replication   = tau^2 / (tau^2 + se_ate_h^2),
    r_perfect       = sqrt(r_replication),
    tost_relative   = map_dbl(0.5 * tau, tost_power, se = se_ate_h),
    tost_fixed_3    = tost_power(3, se_ate_h)
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -tau, decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(
    tau           = "SD of true ATEs (pts)",
    se_reference  = "Reference SE (pts)",
    r_replication = "r (replication)",
    r_perfect     = "r (perfect predictor)",
    tost_relative = "TOST power, relative Δ",
    tost_fixed_3  = "TOST power, fixed Δ = 3"
  )




Table 11: Blinded design analysis (no data touched): expected values of key scoring quantities from design constants (cell sizes ≈500/1,000 per half, outcome SD ≈25) under three plausible spreads of true ATEs. r (replication) is the expected human-replication correlation; r (perfect) the expected raw correlation of an error-free predictor, the square root of the replication value. TOST columns give the per-pair probability that a perfect predictor declares equivalence under the old relative bound and the new fixed bound Δ = 3.

Robustness of the scoring pipeline: split-seed sensitivity
The scoring pipeline has researcher degrees of freedom of its own: chiefly the preregistered random seed that splits the human sample into Human 1 and Human 2, and secondarily the TOST bound. Cummins (2025) show that such analytic choices can move fidelity conclusions substantially, and a skeptic could reasonably ask whether the reported reference value reflects the split we happened to draw. We therefore report a resplit sensitivity check: the half-split is redrawn many times with different seeds, the human replication reference is recomputed on each, and the spread of the reference’s metrics across resplits is reported alongside the primary (preregistered-seed) values. A narrow spread demonstrates that the seed is not driving the results. When real data arrive, the same check is run for the full field: every submission is rescored against each resplit’s Human 1, and the stability of the field summary (and of leaderboard ranks) across resplits is reported.


Code
resplit_ceiling <- cached("amendment_resplit_ceiling", {
  all_ids <- unique(human_data$id)

  map_dfr(1:50, function(i) {
    set.seed(1000 + i)
    ids_i <- sample(all_ids, size = floor(length(all_ids) / 2))
    h1_i  <- human_data |> filter(id %in% ids_i)
    h2_i  <- human_data |> filter(!id %in% ids_i)

    side_of <- function(dat, suffix) {
      map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
        prep_ate(run_main_treatment_model(dat, outcome = out)) |>
          mutate(outcome = out)
      }) |>
        rename_with(~ paste0(.x, suffix), c(estimate, se, p_adj))
    }

    inner_join(side_of(h1_i, "_h"), side_of(h2_i, "_l"),
               by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
      mutate(infer_l = classify_sig(estimate_l, p_adj_l)) |>
      # continuous outcomes only, so raw units are already pp of scale range
      pooled_metrics(include_rmse = TRUE) |>
      mutate(resplit = i)
  })
})

resplit_ceiling |>
  pivot_longer(-resplit, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(metric %in% names(ladder_labels)) |>
  group_by(metric) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            min = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),  max = max(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_defined = sum(!is.na(value)), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(
    # Restricted to the continuous outcomes for comparability with the
    # resplits (whose side_of() maps outcomes_continuous only).
    pairs_list[["Human replication"]] |>
      filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous) |>
      pooled_metrics(include_rmse = TRUE) |>
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric",
                   values_to = "preregistered_seed"),
    by = "metric"
  ) |>
  mutate(metric = factor(unname(ladder_labels[metric]),
                         levels = unname(ladder_labels))) |>
  arrange(metric) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, sd, min, max, preregistered_seed), decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", sd = "SD", min = "Min",
             max = "Max", n_defined = "N defined",
             preregistered_seed = "Preregistered seed")

On placeholder data the reference is itself noise-level, so the check demonstrates the code, not a substantive reference value. The 50 resplits are drawn from the same sample and so are mutually dependent; the spread quantifies split-conditional variability, not independent replications.

When real submissions arrive, both findings are recomputed by replacing the placeholder datasets with the submitted prediction files routed through their tier’s builder (build_pairs_t1() / build_pairs_t2() / build_pairs_t3()). The subgroup and distribution figures are produced for the tiers eligible for them, alongside the demographic-baseline and stereotyping diagnostics of Section 3, including the demographic parity gap (demographic_parity_gap()). The approach-characteristics table coded from the registration forms is then joined to the leaderboard for the exploratory analysis of which design choices accompany better predictions (publicly disclosed features only, with Class C entries excluded from this analysis, not from the leaderboard).

title: The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects
text:
Footnotes


The original experiment contains 20 conditions. The benchmark scores only the 16 non-interactive text interventions. The four left out are the three interactive LLM-chatbot arms and the “Value similarity” quiz, all of which depend on a live back-and-forth that would substantially complicate the simulation task.↩︎
We take Ashokkumar et al.’s megastudy figures, not their headline r = .85, as the reference because the two numbers measure different tasks. The .85 is a correlation over 469 effects pooled from 70 unrelated survey experiments, spanning many topics, treatments, and outcomes. Their megastudy figures instead measure how well the model distinguishes the several interventions of a single megastudy against that study’s shared control, which is exactly this benchmark’s setting (16 interventions, one control). Ashokkumar et al. report the megastudies as a separate secondary archive (15 megastudies, 606 effects) and note that these correlations run lower than the primary archive’s.↩︎

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
library(tidyverse)

library(lubridate)
library(ganttrify)
library(tinytable)

Warning: package 'tinytable' was built under R version 4.4.3
Social scientists are increasingly interested in whether LLMs can mimic the behavior of human subjects. Running surveys and experiments with human participants is costly and takes time. LLM simulations, by contrast, promise a low-cost and fast alternative. If LLMs could reliably approximate human behavior, they would offer substantial new opportunities for social science research: First, the current scientific publishing system mostly favors positive results, which can disencourage the testing of new, disruptive ideas. LLM simulations, could serve as a low-risk tool for pretesting such ideas. Second, theories in social sciences can be highly context dependent. Researchers have called for more systematic exploration of these contexts (almaatouq) and LLM simulations could facilitate this.
Recent studies have showcased LLMs’ potential in simulating public opinion (Kaiser et al. 2025; Wang, Morgenstern, and Dickerson 2025; Argyle et al. 2023; Durmus et al. 2024; Santurkar et al. 2023; Bisbee et al. 2024; Shrestha et al. 2024), and in predicting the outcomes of experiments with human subjects (Chen, Hu, and Lu 2025; Hu et al. 2025; Guo et al. 2025; Lippert et al. 2024; Bisbee et al. 2024, 2024). However, some studies have also highlighted important important caveats, such as misrepresenting minority opinions and lacking diversity.
Making sense of these mixed results is difficult, as most studies face one of the following limitations: First, some studies used already published studies to be predicted by LLMs. The problem is that the LLMs might have been trained on that very data, thus not providing a test for predicting the results of novel studies. Second, even for studies who make a case that they use data which could not have been part of the LLMs training data (e.g. because they used unpublised data), most of these studies were not preregistered. Because LLMs’ performance is dependent on the prompt and the model used, preregistered predictions would make for more credible results. Third, some studies let LLMs simulate human behavior at the experiment level. In these cases, the researchers provided the LLM with a detailed description of the experimental design, and asked it to make a single prediction. These studies might allow to get a sense of average behavior, but it is impossible to investigate heterogenity. Fourth, many studies often focus on narrow sets of interventions and outcomes.
In this project, we will address these issues, by leveraging a unique opportunity to predict a large-scale behavior experiment with LLMs: I am currently leading a megastudy—a large-scale field experiment involving 21’000 participants from the United States (US). The study is about increasing trust in climate scientists. It tests the effects of 20 different text based intervention on several outcomes, including both survey responses (e.g. self-declared trust) and behavioral outcomes (e.g. making a donation to a scientific association). We will use LLMs to simulate individual-level survey responses, using information on population demographics in the US. We will use different models and prompts, and compare their performance. This provides a credible test case on the potential of LLMs to simulate human behavior in survey experiments, across a wide range of interventions and outcome variables.
To evalulate the potential of LLMs , most studies face one or several of the following XX limitations
There are tow main issues with these studies: First, predicitons at the study level.
Making sense of these mixed results is difficult, as the studies vary along many dimensions they do not use the same success metric
studies vary along many dimensions, including, for example, the research topic, the sample, the deployed LLM(s), and the outcome measure.
The aim of this project is to understand how well large language models (LLMs) can simulate human behavior in survey experiments.
By some indicators, LLMs can already mimic human behavior. It has been shown that, in controlled text-based interactions, humans often struggle to distinguish between responses generated by LLMs and those produced by other humans (Jones & Bergen, 2023).
LLMs are trained on extensive human-generated data, and it should come to no patterns of human judgment and reasoning.
LLMs reproduce a wide range of psychological regularities observed in human data, including patterns of judgment, reasoning biases, and social preferences (Binz & Schulz, 2023; Aher et al., 2023).
To some extent, these mixed findings are related to two broad axes along which these studies differ: (i) the level of prediction (study/group-level vs. participant/individual-level), and (ii) the novelty of the intervention (replicating past studies vs. predicting novel studies).
Studies replicating past studies outweigh studies predicting novel studies. The issue is that with these studies, the to-be-predicted study might have been part of the training data. There is a risk that LLM simulations are therefor not predictions, but reproductions of human behavior. To be fair, researchers generally show that their findings are robust to this by different meants
[reference; level of prediction; novelty; main outcome; ]
Individual-level simulations offer greater potential. Predicting novel studies is more a more credible test.
There is a pretty large body of literature studying how well LLMs can simulate human responses. Some of this work has looked at how well LLMs can mirror static opinions (Kaiser et al. 2025), and for which segments of the population (Wang, Morgenstern, and Dickerson 2025; Argyle et al. 2023; Durmus et al. 2024; Santurkar et al. 2023; Bisbee et al. 2024; Shrestha et al. 2024). Other work has tested whether LLMs can predict the outcomes of experiments with human subjects. These studies seem to differ across two broad axes: (i) the level of prediction (study/group-level vs. participant/individual-level), (ii) the novelty of the intervention (replicating human behavior in published studies vs. predicting novel studies).
The studies on predicting novel experiments seem relatively rare, so this might be our research gap. Another research gap might be the outcomes: the megastudy will provide the opportunity to look at several different kinds of outcomes (beliefs/attitudes, but also behavioral outcomes). Maybe existing studies have not really done both?

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The level of prediction
These studies simulate human behavior at the study/group-level: (Chen, Hu, and Lu 2025; Hu et al. 2025; Guo et al. 2025; Lippert et al. 2024; Bisbee et al. 2024, 2024). This means that they predict the average treatment effect of experiments (as well as moderator effects). For example Chen, Hu, and Lu (2025) look at 276 economic field experiments about a wide range of human behaviors. They find that LLMs have a prediction accuracy of 78%. Prediction accuracy is measured as whether the LLM predicts the correct conclusion based on information about the research design, among 3 different conclusion options. They compare only to already published studies’ (but have a successful robustness check on studies published after the stop of the training data of the LLM they used).
These studies simulate human behavior at the participant-level: (Sreedhar et al. 2025; Doudkin, Pataranutaporn, and Maes 2025; Aher, Arriaga, and Kalai 2023; Hewitt et al. 2025; Cui, Li, and Zhou 2024; Yeykelis et al. 2025; Shrestha et al. 2024; Lukumon and Esenogho 2025). This means that they use LLMs to generate simulated agents, intended to mimic human participants. Manning and Horton (2025) provides a good overview of studies on this.

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The novelty of the intervention
Some studies have used LLMs to predict outcomes of already published work or existing data (Chen, Hu, and Lu 2025; Aher, Arriaga, and Kalai 2023; Hu et al. 2025; Hewitt et al. 2025; Guo et al. 2025; Cui, Li, and Zhou 2024; Yeykelis et al. 2025; Bisbee et al. 2024; Lukumon and Esenogho 2025). One problem with these studies is that past data has been included in the LLMs’ training set.
Other studies have tested how well LLMs can simulate human responses in novel studies (Sreedhar et al. 2025; Doudkin, Pataranutaporn, and Maes 2025; Lippert et al. 2024; Shrestha et al. 2024). However, even in these novel studies, sometimes, the predictions were not pre-registered (i.e. did not necessarily happen before data collection) (Sreedhar et al. 2025), or were done after data collection (Lippert et al. 2024).
Among the studies that used simulated agents, some produced individual responses by mimicing different parcitipant demographic profiles [refs] while others did not (Cui, Li, and Zhou 2024)

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The limits of prediction
In general, articles highlighting the potential of LLM in simulating human behavior also point out its limits. For example, several studies suggest that LLMs are overestimating experimental effects on human subjects (Doudkin, Pataranutaporn, and Maes 2025; Cui, Li, and Zhou 2024). Other work suggests that LLMs are bad in making predictions for certain identity groups (Wang, Morgenstern, and Dickerson 2025). Some studies are outright skeptical of the capacity of LLMs in simulating human responses (Schröder et al. 2025).

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Level of analysis
We will make predictions on two levels: the study level and the individual level. For the study level, we will just feed the LLMs information about the research design (methods, participants, stimulus) and let it make a prediction about the study outcome. For the individual level, the LLM simulates fictional individuals (agents). The agents vary in their demographic profile, mimicking a representative sample of the US population.

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The LLMs
We will compare different LLMs. We should probably identify the ones with highest prediction accuracy in other studies.

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The interventions
We will simulate all 107 intervention proposals from the megastudy. In the megastudy, only about 20 intervention proposals will get tested. If the LLM can predict the results for these interventions accurately, then the predictions for the untested interventions are interesting: Are the non-selected interventions worse, or might some in fact be better?

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Training data
There are two potential training data sets for the LLMs:
- https://osf.io/ytf89/overview (from the International Collaboration to Understand Climate Action project)
- https://osf.io/5c3qd (from Viktoria’s TISP project)
The climate action project has the advantage of being intervention data, but not measuring trust in climate scientists in particular. Only the control group in this project also has questions on trust in climate scientists.
Viktoria’s TISP project did not test interventions, but has measured trust more closely to the outcome in the megastudy. We can either use the multi-dimensional measure for trust in scientists (in general), or a one-item question on trust in climate scientists in particular. The first option is more similar in measurement instrument (capturing the same dimensions) while the second option is more similar in concept (climate scientists).
The idea is that this training data will help the LLM understand how different groups, based on their demographic profile, may trust climate scientists. However, the LLM will not know how these groups will react to the interventions.

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
The simulation
LLM simulations are stochastic right? I.e., each run can produce slightly different results. Can we ignore this variation? Or should we address this? If yes, should we set low temperature? Or should we simulate various iterations for each intervention, and then take an average?

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Open questions

How much would this cost? I guess it depends on how many interventions, how big the simulated samples etc. Or we just pick a free-to-use LLM?

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Timeline

Data collection for the Megastudy starts ~in February 2026
Until then, we’d want to have

run the simulations,
written a pre-registration on how exactly we want to compare the results of the simulated agents to the results of the human sample from the megastudy

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Aims and relevance
The aim of this project is to understand how well large language models (LLMs) can simulate human behavior in survey experiments.
Large language models (LLMs) are trained on extensive human-generated data. Consequently, they reproduce patterns of human judgment and reasoning (Aher et al., 2023; Binz & Schulz, 2023). In controlled, text-based interactions, humans sometimes struggle to distinguish between responses generated by LLMs and those produced by other humans (Jones et al., 2025; but see Pagan et al., 2025).
This apparent capability of LLMs to mimic human behavior has generated substantial interest among social scientists. Running surveys and experiments with human subjects is costly and time-consuming. If LLMs could reliably approximate human behavior, they would offer a fast and low-cost alternative. While it seems unrealistic at this point that LLMs could substitute human subjects entirely (see, e.g., Schröder et al., 2025), they might still open up substantial new opportunities for social science research. Academic publishing systems tend to incentivize positive results (Nosek et al., 2012; Smaldino & McElreath, 2016), which can discourage the testing of novel or high-risk ideas. Approximative LLM simulations could serve as a low-risk tool for pretesting such novel ideas before committing resources to human data collection. LLMs could also help testing the boundaries of established theories. Scientific theories—at least in the social sciences—are often highly context-dependent. To improve theory building, researchers have called for more systematic exploration of these contextual variations (Almaatouq et al., 2022). LLMs could make such exploration more feasible: they could simulate a multitude of variations of an experiment, thereby pointing researchers to critical context factors that could be tested in follow-up studies on human participants.
Recent studies have demonstrated promising results for LLMs in simulating public opinion (Argyle et al., 2023; Bisbee et al., 2024; Durmus et al., 2024; Kaiser et al., 2025; Santurkar et al., 2023; Shrestha et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025) and in predicting the outcomes of experiments with human participants (Bisbee et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2025; Hewitt et al., 2025; Hu et al., 2025; Lippert et al., 2024). At the same time, other work has identified important limitations of LLM simulations, including systematic misrepresentation of certain identity groups (Wang et al., 2025), a general tendency of overestimating experimental effects (Cui et al., 2024; Doudkin et al., 2025), and high volatility of simulation results, depending on model and prompt choice (Schröder et al., 2025).
Currently, interpreting these mixed findings is difficult, as most existing studies face at least one of the following limitations. First, some studies rely on already published experiments, raising concerns that LLMs may have been trained on the very data they are asked to simulate. These studies might only test LLMs capacity to reproduce, but not to predict. Second, even studies that plausibly rely on unpublished data are rarely preregistered. Given that LLM performance is highly sensitive to model choice and prompting, preregistered predictions are crucial for credible evaluation. Third, some studies operate at the experiment level, providing LLMs with a full description of the study and requesting a single aggregate prediction. While informative about average effects, this approach precludes the analysis of individual-level heterogeneity.
In this project, we aim to provide a robust test of how well LLMs can simulate human behavior in survey experiments. We address limitations of previous studies by leveraging a unique opportunity to evaluate LLM simulations in the context of a large-scale field experiment. I am currently leading a study involving approximately 21,000 participants from the United States (US), testing 20 text-based interventions designed to increase trust in climate scientists. The study assesses a broad range of outcomes, including self-reported attitudes (e.g., trust in scientists) and behavioral measures (e.g., donations to a scientific organization). Before running the study with human participants, we will use multiple LLMs and prompting strategies to simulate individual-level survey responses representative of the US population. We will then collect the human data and test how closely they match the data simulated by the LLMs. This design allows for a large-scale, credible benchmark test of LLMs’ potential—and limitations—as tools for simulating human subjects in social science research.
For this project, I will collaborate with Sander van der Linden and his team at the Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab, in particular Jon Roozenbeek, Miriam Remshard and Yara Kyrychenko. The lab is one of the world’s leading research groups in the study of digital misinformation and responsible artificial intelligence (AI) systems, with strong expertise in computational social science methods. The team’s technical expertise is central to the successful implementation of this project. The team also provides critical research infrastructure, such as access to high performance clusters and subscriptions to generative AI providers (e.g., ChatGPT).
An in-person visit would greatly benefit this collaboration. Yara Kyrychenko and I are co-leading the study and will work closely together across all stages, including research design, programming, and writing. Sharing a physical workspace will not only facilitate coordination—it will provide learning opportunities that would not occur in a remote setting. My personal goal from this collaboration is to broaden my methodological toolkit. Instead of outsourcing complex computational tasks to Cambridge team, I aim to be directly involved in their implementation. Only an in-person visit would allow me to be truly embedded in the Cambridge team’s daily workflows.


Methods
The project combines large-scale experimental survey data (N = 21,000) with extensive computational simulations using LLMs. We will simulate individual-level survey responses by prompting the LLMs with varying demographic characteristics, achieving representativeness of the U.S. population. We will compare multiple LLMs and use different prompting strategies to evaluate how well they mimic the observed human data. Our evaluation criteria include average treatment effects, response distributions, subgroup heterogeneity, and systematic biases (e.g., misrepresentation of demographic groups).
The simulations that we are planning will be computationally intensive, as they require generating individual-level responses to many different interventions, across several outcome variables, using different LLMs ( OpenAI’s GPT-4.1, Google’s Gemini 2.0 and Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Anthropic’s Claude 3.5 and Claude Sonnet 4), as well as multiple prompts. We will simulate LLM responses via an API, querying the models directly (e.g., GPT-4) rather than using commercial web-based chat applications (e.g., ChatGPT). ChatGPT is a chatbot designed for broad commercial use and includes additional features, such as internet access, which the underlying GPT-4 model itself does not have. To run the simulations, we will rely on computational infrastructure at Cambridge University, in particular the high performance computing platforms offered by the Cambridge Service for Data-Driven Discovery (CSD3).
This project—as all my other research projects—commits to open science practices. Both the experimental study and the LLM simulations will be preregistered. All data, code, materials, and analyses will be made publicly available.
I will lead the integration of experimental and simulated data and conduct the main statistical analyses. Yara Kyrychenko will lead the implementation of the LLM simulations, including model selection, prompt design, and bias evaluation. All team members, including those not in the lead, will be closely involved at all project stages.


Schedule
Figure 1 illustrates the timeline for this research project. We will complete the preregistration of the LLM simulations and collect data for the large-scale field experiment on strengthening trust in climate scientists before the start of the research stay. At the start of the research stay, detailed simulation protocols and human survey responses will therefore already be available.
During the six-week research stay, we will begin by running the LLM simulations (weeks one and two). We will then analyze and compare the LLM simulated data with the human data (weeks three and four). Here, too, we will be able to rely on our detailed preregistrations, including code for statistical analyses, which makes two weeks a realistic estimate. During the last three weeks of the stay (from week four until week six), we will draft a first version of the manuscript.
After the research stay, we will include feedback from collaborators and revise the manuscript accordingly. Within six weeks after the research stay, we will submit the manuscript to a peer-reviewed journal and simultaneously publish the manuscript as a publicly available preprint on the Open Science Framework (OSF).

Expected outcomes

Extending my research agenda and learning new technical skills
This project sets an important new milestone in my personal research agenda. In my work, I’m trying to understand how people form accurate beliefs about the world. During my PhD, I have investigated how susceptible people are to misinformation (Pfänder & Altay, 2025), and to which extent people accept scientific information (Pfänder, Kerzreho, et al., 2025). Because what be believe to be true depends a lot on whom we trust, I have developed a cognitive explanation of why people come to trust science, despite not knowing much of it (Pfänder, Rouilhan, et al., 2025; Pfänder, De Courson, et al., 2025; Pfänder & Mercier, 2025). Currently, during my first postdoc year, I’m continuing research on trust in science (Pfänder et al., 2026), with a particular applied focus on strengthening people’s trust in climate science. In parallel, I am trying to extend my research agenda to artificial intelligence: To which extent do people trust LLMs, and how does this affect the accuracy of people’s beliefs? The LLM simulation project proposed here is a first step in that new direction.
To study human–AI interactions rigorously, I need to further develop my computational skill set. In my previous work, I have employed a broad range of methods and research designs, including survey experiments, meta-analyses, and simulation-based approaches. All of my publications are implemented as fully computationally reproducible workflows, in line with best practices in open science. However, I have a lot to learn yet about more programming-intensive approaches, such as agent-based modeling, cloud-based computation, and collaborative software development using shared codebases—-necessary technical skills to conduct cutting edge research on human-AI interactions. Working closely with the Cambridge team will allow me to acquire these skills.


Peer-reviewed publication
I will be shared first author of the resulting scientific publication, together with Yara Kyrychenko. Given the broad social science audience of our research project, we will target high-impact general science journals such as Nature Human Behaviour, PNAS, or Science Advances. If these outlets do not accept our manuscript, we will prioritize journals that value methodological contributions, such as Behavior Research Methods or Nature Computational Science, which have recently published similar work on LLM simulations of behavioral data (e.g., Cui et al., 2025).


Expanding my network
The visit will support my long-term academic development by embedding me in a leading international research environment at the Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab. Being based in Cambridge will enable intellectual exchange with other researchers beyond the host lab, including Ramit Debnath, whose work on human-centric AI systems for climate action closely aligns with the research agenda of my current postdoc.
Even beyond Cambridge, I will be able to expand my professional network: Cambridge is situated close to other leading research centers on studying artificial intelligence (AI) and digital communication, such as the Oxford Internet Institute and the Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism. This proximity provides opportunities for additional exchanges. Specifically, I plan on visiting Richard Fletcher and Felix Simon at the Reuters Institute, and present our work there during a lab meeting. These interactions will help me position myself in the community of computational social scientists studying human-AI interactions.



Collaboration and partnership
My research visit will provide substantial benefits both for my own academic development and for the Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab.
Beyond acquiring new methodological skills and expanding my professional network (see above), spending time in a well-established, internationally renowned lab at the University of Cambridge will strengthen my institutional academic mobility—an important factor for my future career. The visit would also fruitfully complement my current experience working in a newly founded research group at Eawag of which I am currently the only group member besides my PI. This exchange will therefore allow me to gain insights into the work dynamics of a larger research group.
The research stay, I believe, will also be beneficial to the Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab. I will contribute expertise in survey design, statistical analysis, and open science practices, as well as provide access to a unique, high-powered field experiment on trust in climate scientists. This dataset offers a rare opportunity to evaluate LLM-based simulations of human behavior and directly aligns with the lab’s interests in human–technology interaction and the societal implications of AI systems.
The research exchange will further strengthen ongoing and future planned research collaborations between the lab in Cambridge and the group I am part of at Eawag. Both groups have a strong overlap in research interests, providing a solid foundation for future collaboration. For example, I am currently leading a meta-analysis on the effectiveness of behavioral interventions to protect people from falling for misinformation—work that closely aligns with ongoing research at the Cambridge Social Decision-Making Lab. A visit by a member of the Cambridge team to Eawag is already planned for 2027.
